title: 8 Wineries Around the World That Are Basically Sculpture Museums
summary:
Pause to swirl your glass while admiring the striking sculptures at these wineries.

The post 8 Wineries Around the World That Are Basically Sculpture Museums appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
It’s not uncommon for people who love wine to also love art.  So, it’s only natural that wineries around the world are serving fresh-air wine and sculpture pairings, with on-site sculpture gardens, outdoor galleries, and artful walking trails unfurling through the vines. For the visitor, these sip, stroll, and sculpture experiences become multi-sensory explorations of the interplay between land and art and wine, celebrating the creativity of both the winemaker and the artist. 

Meander through these eight wineries near and far that showcase both wine and sculpture.

Pt. Leo Estate

Merricks, Victoria, Australia

The owners of Mornington Peninsula’s Pt. Leo Estate share their personal collection of large-scale sculptures in an ever-evolving, 16-acre Sculpture Park, which welcomes approximately 200,000 visitors each year. The outdoor gallery boasts a stellar group of 70-plus permanent, large-scale works by Australian and international artists including Yayoi Kusama, Henry Moore, Reko Rennie, Ugo Rondinone, Jaume Plensa, Barry Flanagan, Inge King, Antony Gormley, and KAWS. While enjoying sweeping Southern Ocean vistas, guests can try a glass of Pt. Leo Estate’s Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, or Pinot Gris before joining a guided tour of the sculptures.

Château de Vullierens

Vullierens, Switzerland

Château de Vullierens has belonged to the same family for over 700 years, and these days it’s also a certified biodynamic vineyard featuring an open-air sculpture park with over 120 works of contemporary art woven into the estate’s 74 acres of gardens, forests, and vineyards. Bonus: The whole thing overlooks Lake Geneva and the Alps. There’s even an interactive sculpture trail designed just for kids. “Both wine and sculpture invite discovery, curiosity, and exchange,” says Julie Haering, project manager at Château de Vullierens. “Just as each wine expresses a terroir, each artwork interacts with the landscape, the light, the seasons, and the visitor’s personal interpretation.”

The Donum Estate

Sonoma, USA

Wine, sustainable agriculture, and art unite within the cohesive vision of The Donum Estate, where the world-class Donum Collection includes over 60 monumental works displayed over 200 acres of land. Visitors are encouraged to engage with nature (and sip Donum Chardonnay and Pinot Noir) while they peruse sculptures by renowned artists including Keith Haring, Yayoi Kusama, Ai Weiwei, Robert Indiana, Anselm Kiefer, Fernando Botero, and Louise Bourgeois. A range of immersive tour experiences, from guided walking to all-terrain vehicles, are the perfect way to see Donum’s culinary gardens, working farm, and art collection.

Brick Bay

Matakana, New Zealand

Up for a walk? In the Matakana wine region of New Zealand, Brick Bay offers a two-kilometer Sculpture Trail. It’s an open-air exhibition of contemporary New Zealand art, with an ever-changing collection of over 60 large-scale sculptures scattered across a natural landscape with vineyards, lakes, open pastures, a kauri forest, and native bush. While tasting Brick Bay’s Pinot Gris, Chardonnay, rosé, or Bordeaux blends, guests might hear the birdsong of a kākā or tūī as they roam among sculptures that move, float, or interact with the sun and wind.

Exclusive Access to
Wine Country,
Led by Wine Enthusiast

Artelium Wine Estate

East Sussex, England

Art is in the DNA of Artelium Wine Estate, where visitors encounter a growing collection of contemporary sculpture displayed within 45 verdant acres on the edge of the South Downs. “We wanted to create a place where wine, art, and nature could exist together, so the sculpture program evolved very naturally from that vision,” says Julie Bretland, Artelium co-founder. “Wine is such a cultural product—it’s shaped by creativity, craftsmanship, patience, and place—and sculpture offers another way for people to connect with that.” Along with permanent sculpture installations, the winery hosts temporary exhibitions and supports artist collaborations, an artist-in-residence program, and even stone carving workshops.

Montalto

Red Hill South, Victoria, Australia

Visitors to Montalto’s 1.2-kilometer Sculpture Trail are invited to grab a glass of wine and take off through the vines, into the woods, and along wetland boardwalks to discover a rotating collection of over 40 contemporary works of sculpture. “There’s a beautiful resonance between sculpture and the work of the vineyard,” says Simon Black, Montalto’s chief winemaker. “You’re tasting the organic creativity of the winemaker and encountering the organic creativity of the sculptors at the same time, and every sense is engaged: the sight of the landscape, the scent of the gardens and the vines, the taste of the wine.”

Paradise Ridge Winery

Sonoma, USA

At Paradise Ridge, guests can savor award-winning wines and Russian River Valley vistas before taking a walk through Marijke’s Grove, a four-acre sculpture garden launched in 1996 that features both a permanent collection and an ongoing schedule of rotating shows. Large-scale pieces include David Best’s 25-ton “Temple of Remembrance” and Laura Kimpton’s 12-foot-tall “LOVE” sculpture. Fun fact: The winery also gives a second life to artworks that previously debuted at Burning Man.

“Wine is one way that people connect with the land, and sculpture is another,” says Paradise Ridge’s Maria Juliana Byck. “At its heart, the sculpture program is about creating a place where people can enjoy nature, art, and wine together in a way that feels contemplative, inspiring, and rejuvenating.”

Château La Coste

Provence, France

With a winery and welcome building designed by Jean Nouvel and Tadao Ando, respectively, Château La Coste is a treasure trove of artistic brilliance. Along with olive groves, a forest, vegetable garden, and vineyard, the 494-acre property houses 45 sculpture installations, including both temporary and permanent exhibitions. On a guided two-hour Art & Architecture Walk, visitors meander over hills, through the woods, and into the vineyards while admiring four kilometers of creative expression, including works by Louise Bourgeois, Frank Gehry, Yoko Ono, Tracey Emin, Ai Weiwei, Richard Serra, Sophie Calle, Kengo Kuma, and even an ironwork freight car by Bob Dylan.

title: How to Stand Out in a Bouquet of Rosés
summary:
Winemakers are using playful and inventive bottle shapes to distinguish themselves on a shelf full of pink.

The post How to Stand Out in a Bouquet of Rosés appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Other than its namesake blush coloring, the limits on what constitutes rosé are few: neither specific regions nor particular grapes necessarily define the category, and myriad winemaking techniques can result in pink juice. Certain regions are, of course, well-known for rosé, including Provence and France’s rosé-only Tavel appellation, and many winemakers are indeed producing varietally-designated rosés. For a category that has been gaining rapid traction since #RoséAllDay was born over a decade ago, however, it seems most consumers pay little attention to what rosé actually is—other than refreshing and pink.

“When I speak to consumers, nobody knows [how it’s made], and it’s not very important for them, I think,” says Olivier Souvelain, CEO of Maison Gassier in Provence. “Everyone is surprised that it’s not simply a blend between red and white.” 

“I’m not sure most consumers fully understand how rosé is made,” concurs Emma Bertrand, creative director of Gerard Bertrand’s Cote des Roses. “But is that really necessary to enjoy it? Today, rosé is less about technical knowledge and more about what it represents: a lifestyle, a mood, a destination,” she says. “I think people don’t necessarily choose a rosé for its grape variety, but for how it makes them feel.” 

This thinking is consistent with evidence presented during a recent Wine Paris session entitled “How Consumers Choose a Bottle of Wine: What Neuroscience Reveals About Packaging.” Isabelle Gruard, of packaging leader Crealis, and neuroscientist Andrea Ciceri, of data analytics resource Sensecatch, shared statistics that speak to this very phenomenon: research shows 95% of daily decisions are driven by the emotional system of the brain, while only 5% are based on rational thinking. Consumers spend all of about 15 seconds in front of a shelf making a decision, with any given product only getting a few fleeting seconds to capture attention.

Grabbing Attention with Rosé-Colored Glasses

Given that rosé is such a lifestyle-driven category, this feelings-driven decision-making is perhaps more relevant than with any other wine style. “Rosé is inherently visual,” says Max Rohn, CEO of Wölffer Estate Vineyard. “It’s about color and emotion, so the bottle becomes an extension of that experience.”

“In more traditional wine segments, consumers often navigate by appellation, producer reputation, or vintage,” Riccardo Pasqua, CEO of Pasqua Wines, points out. “In rosé, those markers carry less weight for most buyers. What drives the decision is far more instinctive—a shape that catches the eye, a label that provokes curiosity, or a name that sticks.”

On that score, savvy rosé producers are using enhanced visual or mnemonic cues beyond color to stand out in an increasingly crowded field. “Rosé is one of the most competitive and visually-driven categories in wine,” says Rohn. “On a shelf full of pale pink, differentiation becomes critical.”

Producers use this notion to their advantage. Wölffer Estate’s flower-adorned rosé, rebranded to Summer in a Bottle, has grown from just over 1500 cases in 2014 to tens of thousands of cases annually. Pasqua’s shapely and playfully-named 11 Minutes represents 10% of the Veneto-based brand’s total profile, with its strongest sales among on-trade, lifestyle-driven settings like hotel bars and beach clubs. “[These are settings] where the bottle has a life beyond the shelf,” says Pasqua. “It sits on a table and other guests see it; it travels around a room.”

Distinctive in both bottle design and name, Bertrand’s Cote des Roses “has become one of the most recognizable French rosé brands internationally, particularly in the U.S.,” says Bertrand, with sales figures that approach the perennially popular Château d’Esclans Whispering Angel. Finally, Maison Gassier’s sales of its estate rosé grew by over 40% in 2025, with all other factors being equal year-over-year, simply by rolling out a new hexagonal bottle shape.

Silhouettes in Rosé

Bottle shape has always played a significant part in the rosé category, especially in Provence, with the introduction of various come-hither silhouettes. Most notably, the consortium Syndicat de Défense des Côtes de Provence (now known as AOC Côtes de Provence) introduced a signature bottle for the region in the 1960s: the voluptuous “flûte à corset Provençal.”

Such bottles acted not only as markers of differentiation, but also as signifiers of quality. “Provençal roses are some of the most expensive all over the world,” acknowledges Souvelain. “It’s like perfume. When you sell something expensive, you try to prove [visually] that what’s inside is of a different quality than what others are offering.”

At Wine Paris, the Provençal rosé section alone revealed dozens of bottle shapes, even among wines with a generally similar makeup. It’s no wonder that even legacy producers such as 50-year-old Gassier would want to explore a new look. Its new hexagonal bottle is about more than just style, though—it tells a story, with six sides meant to reflect the brand’s six generations of winemakers. The bottle also shoulders the burden of price justification with the new design’s implied added value. “Before we launched the new bottle we did a study with 2,000 consumers between France and the U.S.,” says Souvelain. “In both markets, nearly everyone surveyed said that they would agree to pay even just one euro or one dollar more per bottle for the new shape.”

Meanwhile, Pasqua’s bottle features broad shoulders compared to most in the rosé category, but without excessive flourish. “We wanted a silhouette that felt distinctive and premium without being decorative for its own sake,” says Pasqua. And Cote des Roses’ bottle, distinctive in its heavy-bottomed, rose-embossed punt and glass cork, “creates a very strong in-store interaction,” says Bertrand. “People notice it, pick it up, and when they enjoy the wine, they easily recognize it the next time. That physical connection naturally drives repeat purchases. It’s a wine that people don’t just drink, they share it, photograph it, and gift it.”

title: Go Ahead—Judge Wine by Its Label
summary:
Wine labels are becoming more Instagrammable, expressive, and fun as producers catch up to what young drinkers want.

The post Go Ahead—Judge Wine by Its Label appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
After a string of TikToks touted La Vieille Ferme Rosé in 2024, Famille Perrin, owners of Château de Beaucastel, were faced with a conundrum. Consumers—particularly newer, younger ones—didn’t know how to pronounce the name of their wine. Instead, internet minions were calling the decades-old brand—which has earned widespread recognition for being an approachable, inexpensive and consistently well-made rosé—“chicken wine,” so-named because of the chicken illustration on the label.

But rather than letting it bristle their feathers, the makers leaned in. A few months later, they launched a new limited-edition label. The words “La Vieille Ferme Rosé” are nowhere to be found; in their place, “The Chicken Wine” is prominently emblazoned. The rebrand has so far delighted TikTok users across the U.S. and U.K., who have embraced the new label.

“Wine can still be such a scary, antiquated ‘white tablecloth’ category for so many people,” says Sande Friedman, a wine writer based in Philadelphia. “Younger consumers are far more vocal about that than previous generations … and admit openly that we shop with our eyes and are more open to change than previous generations may have been. A generation of people shopping for ‘the chicken wine’ really showed that power.”

The Power of Visuals

Indeed, the Chicken Wine phenomenon proves, once again, that visuals matter, whether we want to admit it or not. In turn, wine manufacturers are placing new weight on what goes on a label, from the illustrations and typeface to wine descriptors and buzzwords.

“Instructional buzzwords can actually help inform customers of how the winemaker would enjoy this product themselves,” says Friedman. “‘Chillable’ is a word you see a lot … that helps inform people that red wine can be chilled.”

And colorful labels with playful fonts aren’t just frivolous details. Much like a shop’s window dressing can lure in foot traffic, these eye-catching labels can be the difference between making a sale and, well, not. Consumer research backs this up, suggesting that both colors and fonts play significant roles in reeling in new customers. “There’s been an undeniable shift in the look of wine labels in recent years,” Friedman says. “It’s in tandem with what you see happening in soda, snacks, all the ‘fun’ food categories. [There are] bright colors, buzzwords, flash and pomp.”

A Generational Shift

The shift in buying habits is notedly generational. For many millennial and Gen Z consumers, demand for wine has shifted, softened, and evolved in a post-pandemic timeline, says Kathryn Klos, a North Carolina-based sommelier turned travel advisor specializing in wine tourism. 

“For younger generations, who are often less wealthy, experienced and perhaps less ‘serious’ about wine, a playful or artful label signals approachability,” she says. “They know their friends or followers might not know the difference between Montepulciano and Brunello di Montalcino, but they sure as hell know a cute label.”

Klos says this isn’t to minimize the wine knowledge of younger generations. “But the truth is, fewer of us can afford to drink wine as a serious hobby the way older generations could,” she stresses. From a stocking perspective, listening to this customer feedback is critical for winemakers looking to stay relevant.

“It’s a very important driver in the current market,” says Friedman, explaining that some importers are supporting the design of tiny producers’ fun labels to help little cuvées from native grapes stand out.

It’s also convincing more makers to incorporate UPC barcodes into their labels, which can help streamline inventory management and more accurately track products throughout the supply chain. “Big, huge distributors are noticing and copying, which is why you see this everywhere now,” she says.

New Styles, New Label Best Practices

Natural wines—a broad category without a firm definition—are leading the modern label charge. One possible explanation: Many producers, especially those in Europe, are legally prohibited from naming the wine’s regional designation or the specific vineyard in which it was made. (These wines are often deemed “atypical” for their region and are excluded from these designations.) 

Without being able to capitalize on such selling points, many winemakers instead lean on untraditional, eye-catching branding. Case in point, the delicate, floral- inspired labels from Jurtschitsch, a natural wine producer in Austria’s Kamptal region, and the whimsical, cartoonish labels from Domaine de l’Octavin, a Demeter biodynamic-certified outfit in the Arbois region of the Jura.

There’s plenty of great natural wine out there, made by vintners with high-quality standards. But as hype for natural wine continues to trend, shelves are becoming saturated with natural wines of varying quality, many of them bearing flashy labels slapped with phrases like “low sulfur,” “naked,” and “sugar-free.” 

Critics say these health-adjacent buzzwords are unhelpful at best and misleading at worst. There are scant legal regulations around their use, which, coupled with sexy label art, can confuse consumers, particularly those unfamiliar with classic winemaking. As a result, more faulty wines with off-putting flavors or aromas are entering the marketplace.

“There was a moment in natural wine when labels alone could get you on a shelf and sell bottles,” says Klos. “And unfortunately, the wine inside was bad more often than it should have been.”

The Good vs. Bad of New Labels

So, does the good outweigh the bad in the great label debate? Friedman says cool branding can help mask cruddy wine, but it can also push boundaries in an industry that’s often stuck in its ways.

“Anything that makes people more comfortable shopping is a win for an industry that’s been in a downward trend in recent years,” she says. “It also actually helps people remember wines they’ve enjoyed better, because there’s an easy visual instead of trying to remember a grape or place.”

Plus, Klos says that a fun label means a consumer is more likely to deem a bottle “gram worthy” and perhaps give it some free marketing online. “More often than not, a nice bottle is a splurge—and one that deserves to be documented on social media,” she says. “The game has changed for the next generation of wine drinkers, and art has a lot of power in that game.”

What Makes a Good Label?

Wine consultant Kathryn Klos shares what makes for an effective label these days.

Buzzwords Beyond Buzzwords’ Sake

“When there’s full transparency from a winemaker or brand about farming practices, regenerative choices and winemaking intention, that’s a beautiful thing. But when big brands use unregulated phrases like ‘no chemicals added’ without doing anything different just to make more money, we run into problems.”

Aesthetically Pleasing, but Authentic

“Yes, the label should be art-forward, but art without purpose feels empty and people can sniff that out. There’s nothing wrong with beauty, but when art becomes detached from intention, it risks losing its soul. A wine label can be wild and whimsical, but if it doesn’t echo the spirit of what’s inside, it becomes costume instead of character.”

Storytelling with Honesty

“Just like a great wine reflects its maker and its place, good label copy is an opportunity to extend the story and make the experience more memorable. Ultimately, the intended audience and their values should dictate what that means.”

This article originally appeared in the June/July 2025 California issue of Wine Enthusiast magazine. Click here to subscribe today!

title: These 15 Frozen Cocktails Prove That Slushies Aren’t Just for Kids
summary:
A piña colada by the pool? A sophisticated slushie after dinner? Or a frozen espresso martini to get the night started? Take your pick with our favorite frozen cocktail recipes.

The post These 15 Frozen Cocktails Prove That Slushies Aren’t Just for Kids appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Frozen drinks can be polarizing. To a certain extent, the haters have a point. There are many ill-fated iterations of slushies that range from boozy and headache-inducing to icy and cloyingly sweet. But we’re here to tell you it doesn’t have to be this way. 

When done right, frozen drinks are the quintessential grown-up slushie. The best are supremely refreshing while packing a punch. Plus, they can easily be tweaked to personal preferences for strength, sweetness, consistency, and acidity. In the summertime, they are an obvious choice for all poolside, seaside, or rooftop occasions and beyond. 

But even in the depths of winter, these cool cocktails can transport us to fantasies of warm weather and sunshine. Contrary to popular belief, frozen cocktails don’t have to be tropical and fruit-forward. There are plenty of recipes that lean into the milkshake vibes, and they are satisfyingly decadent in any season. 

We’ve rounded up our favorite frozen drink recipes for when you need a little something special to sip on. Yes, they’re a little indulgent—but all the best things in life are.

The Classics

Piña Colada

The piña colada is a classic for a reason. White, dark, and coconut rums meet pineapple juice and coconut cream for an easy-drinking slush that’ll make you feel like you’re on vacation no matter the time or place.

Frozen Painkiller

The Painkiller adds a splash of orange juice to the classic piña colada flavor profile for a citrusy hit, and this frozen variation is as tropical and dreamy as can be.

Frozen Hemingway Daiquiri

The frozen Hemingway Daiquiri just might be as tiki as it gets. With two types of rum and a smidge of Maraschino liqueur, it’s lightly sweet and perfectly boozy. Plus—the addition of grapefruit juice means it’s practically made for breakfast or brunch.

Frozen Gin & Tonics

This recipe for frozen G&Ts uses tonic syrup to replicate the classic tonic water flavors. It’s bitter, lightly sweet, and ultra refreshing with a touch of citrus.

Brandy Slush

You don’t need to be in Wisconsin to enjoy the classic brandy slush with this no-frills, no-blender recipe. With a little planning ahead and a combination of brandy, fruit juices, and black tea, this midwest aperitivo is served.

The Twists

Baller Margarita

This frozen margarita variation also nods to the Sgroppino with the addition of lime sherbet and Champagne (although any sparkling wine will do). A splash of Licor 43 adds sweetness and spice.

San Jose Frosé

The San Jose frosé blitzes up rosé with frozen lemonade cubes and lemon juice for a tart twist on the classic frozen rosé. 

Whiskey Sour Slush

Love whiskey sours but don’t love using raw egg whites at home? Want something a bit better suited for the sweltering summer? This whiskey sour slush is the answer. Plus—no blender required!

Frozen Strawberry Riesling Cocktail

Listen, if you don’t like sweet drinks, you might want to skip this strawberry and riesling slush, as it definitely leans into the fruitier side of things. Still, the strawberry and lime paired with the delicate Riesling is almost irresistible.

Sophisticated Slushie

This sophisticated slushie blends the tequila and grapefruit flavors of a Paloma with the texture and sparkling wine technique of an Italian Sgroppino. It’s bright and refreshing—the perfect sipper all summer long.

Ube Colada

Kara Newman describes this Ube Colada as “a piña colada with a Filipino accent.” The purple yam’s vanilla flavors meld harmoniously with the piña colada’s pineapple-coconut profile. 

Jack Frost Cocktail

Sometimes, looks can be deceiving, as is the case with this tropical and fruity Jack Frost cocktail. It may look festive and creamy due to the blue Curaçao and snow-like coconut rim, but, with rum, pineapple juice, and coconut water, it’s actually more like a piña colada in holiday mode. 

The Boozy Milkshakes

Grand Cayman Mudslide

The Mudslide just might be the most essential, creamy frozen drink. Equal parts of vodka (vanilla-flavored, if you’re feeling fancy), Kahlua, and Baileys are blitzed together to a sweet and luscious treat. 

The Bushwacker

The Bushwacker is a delicious marriage between a White Russian, piña colada, and mudslide. It might sound like a lot of flavors at once, but the creamy coconut and coffee liqueur balance each other well. Try out vodka in place of the rum or a splash of orange liqueur for a bright twist.

Frozen Espresso Martini

The simple blend of vodka, coffee, and simple syrup keeps this frozen espresso cocktail refreshing and straightforward. To make it even more decadent, add a bit of Baileys.

title: Will New York City’s New Anti-Alcohol Campaign Actually Empty Glasses?
summary:
The controversial campaign to educate New Yorkers about the negative health risks of wine, beer, and cocktails is alarming hospitality pros.

The post Will New York City’s New Anti-Alcohol Campaign Actually Empty Glasses? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Early in May, a new type of beer poster was wheatpasted on New York City’s bus stops, subway platforms, bar walls, and bathrooms. They even popped up on Instagram feeds. But these ads weren’t for Heineken or Brooklyn Brewery. Instead, the featured beer labels read, “Cancer Risk”.

Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s new campaign, “Buzzkill,” is aimed at educating New Yorkers about the risks associated with alcohol consumption—some studies have found there is strong evidence that correlates several forms of cancer and heavy alcohol consumption.

“New Yorkers deserve to know the truth about the risk of cancer when drinking alcohol,” says Dr. Alister Martin, New York City’s newly-appointed Health Commissioner. 

But industry members find the campaign alarmist and, frankly, scary as hell—specifically, using the c-word to get New Yorkers on the wagon. Moreso, critics argue the ads favor fear-mongering reports, and forgo the scientific nuance of alcohol-and-health arguments. 

Are scare tactics the right approach for getting New Yorkers to engage with alcohol more responsibly? And will these posters impact the already-slim margins of the city’s struggling hospitality industry? 

But the biggest question is, is this a sign of similar policies to come?

Unpacking the Policies

The campaign’s primary message is: alcohol is a known carcinogen, and drinking less will lower your risk of cancer. 

“To some our data may be a buzzkill, but it’s actually their buzz that can kill,” says Dr. Martin’s statement. “The good news is that drinking less lowers the risk of cancer, and knowing the facts means you’re in control.”

It’s the first time any major American city has used cancer—not drinking and driving, underage drinking, or general debauchery—as their temperance campaign’s central focus.

Other international cities and countries have pursued similar efforts.

Ireland has warnings on cans and bottles connecting alcohol and fatal cancers. South Australia plastered subway cars with glasses of red wine, with the accompanying text “alcohol causes cancer in seven sites of the body.”

Mamdani’s campaign announcement went even further, and acted as a bouncing-off point for State Senator Nathalia Fernandez to advocate for a tax on alcohol, which will fund prevention, education, and rehabilitation efforts. 

When you compare Mayor Mamdani’s campaign with the current Trump administration’s guidance, things get complicated. 

The 2025 to 2030 U.S. Dietary Guidelines, released earlier this year by the Trump administration, scrapped the recommendations that women and men should have no more than one, and two, drinks per day, respectively.

Moreover, recent research from the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine (NASEM), which was used to inform the Dietary Guidelines, found that “moderate alcohol consumption” can lower risks of cardiovascular disease and all-cause mortality. However, the report did connect moderate alcohol consumption with a higher risk of breast cancer. 

Meanwhile, the World Health Organization (WHO) takes an opposite stance, stating that no amount of alcohol is safe for human consumption. Conflicting and confusing? Yes, but don’t worry: We’ve catalogued the full breakdown of health and alcohol’s ongoing facts and theories.

What Experts Think

Of course, industry experts agree that it is important consumers are aware of potential health risks. 

Excessive drinking is highly dangerous, hard stop. The New York State Department of Health estimates over 8,050 New Yorkers die each year due to excessive alcohol use, and one in six adults across the state participate in binge or heavy drinking. 

Alcohol consumption can nominally raise your risk for several different types of cancer by 1 to 2%, as do genetics, diet, smoking, and other environmental factors.

But, critics take issue with how the campaign stuck the landing. The posters flag that alcohol—all alcohol—has cancer risks. It’s a binary argument with a dark insinuation: Your evening plans, whether that’s 20 beers with the boys or a glass of wine with dinner, could have terminal outcomes.

“There’s a wide gap between encouraging mindful consumption and a campaign like this built on loosely-interpreted facts to create fear,” says David Skurnik, president of Skurnik Wines & Spirits.

Colin Spoelman, founder and distiller of Kings County Distillery, agrees, saying, “There’s no sense of the relative harm of alcohol in these posters. Is it bad for you like smoking? Like hot dogs? Like sugary drinks?”

Spoelman doesn’t understand why alcohol is the local villain here, while the aforementioned vices are all equally antagonized by the World Health Organization. Why is New York putting drinks in its crosshairs?

“If the mayor won’t lay out the harms of hot dogs for New York City residents, then he is playing favorites,” says Spoelman.

Torrey Grant, wine director at Leonetta and The Derby Club, seconds this, and points out that the increased relative risk of colorectal cancer from two glasses of wine per day is 7%. 50 grams of processed meat—about the size of a standard hot dog—will increase relative risk by 18%.

“Can you imagine going after the BEC or a pastrami sandwich at Katz Deli as a carcinogen!?” says Grant. “The BEC is fuel for the working New Yorker. The Katz Pastrami is a foundational food for this city. But it doesn’t absolve them of being carcinogenic, does it?“

It’s not that these industry pros are against health warnings, per se—they’re for fairness. “I would favor updating federal health warning statements to be more specific,” says Spoelman. “But at a city level, it seems misplaced.”

Given the backlash, it begs the question: Is this really the right public health battle for the mayor’s office to tackle?

“I believe that NY’s new ‘anti-buzz’ campaign is a misdirected effort to improve the health of NY’ers—which, in itself, is a valid goal,” says Wendy Weinstein of The Newport Restaurant Group. “There are greater threats to everyday health and risk of long term disease for New Yorkers: omnipresent bad food options, food scarcity, nutritional education, and food deserts.”

Rather than “the pinpointing of micro factors,” she believes, “a comprehensive overhaul of the foodways of many NY’ers is needed to combat cancer.”

Glasses Half Empty

Both the restaurant and alcohol industries are suffering through myriad challenges, like ever-slim margins, supply chain issues, and changing consumer preferences, and it’s natural to think that this campaign might make matters worse.

Gillian Garrett is an alcohol regulatory attorney. She agrees the campaigns are alarmist, and she expects them to lead to a decrease in alcohol consumption. 

“When people are told a product causes cancer, they use less of it,” says Garrett. 

Elia Chuaqui, a sommelier at Hudson Local, expects to see that reflected in sales, especially if Senator Fernandez’ tax is to move forward. To Chuaqui, affordability will worsen, resulting in selling fewer glasses per visit, while having to focus on more value-driven selections and a wider array of by-the-glass options.

“In the short term, we would expect some softening in demand,” says Chuaqui. “Long term, the impact is harder to predict.” 

Wine consumption is deeply social, especially in New York. People come to New York for the pedigree of bars, restaurants, and culture. 

“Dining, hospitality, tourism, and small businesses are such an important part of New York’s community fabric,” says Skurnik. “Responsible wine consumption as a shared experience is a big part of what makes the city special and vibrant.” 

Here is where the problem lies: “Reducing wine to a public health warning ignores its enormous contribution to our cultural values,” he adds.

title: Pinot Noir Lovers Are Falling for This Trending Greek Grape
summary:
Xinomavros’s energy, complexity, and finesse place it alongside some of the world’s most prestigious red wines, including Burgundy and Nebbiolo.

The post Pinot Noir Lovers Are Falling for This Trending Greek Grape appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Xinomavro, Greece’s signature indigenous grape from the northern region of Macedonia, often makes a striking impression on newcomers. 

From the first sip, it’s clear this is a wine unlike any other. Red-cherry core, sun-dried tomato, oregano, and a distinct stony dustiness unfold into a savory aromatic profile that transports us directly to its place of origin.

“Xinomavro offers a seductive aroma that has genuine depth,” says Yiannis Karakasis, Master of Wine. “While many aromatic red wines exude charm, Xinomavro combines vibrant aromas with high natural acidity and a robust tannic structure, making it feel both delicate and well-structured.”

Its relatively transparent ruby color can be misleading—an iron-like grip emerges on the palate, yet it remains pleasurable, shaped by fine, sandpaper tannins.

“Part of what makes Xinomavro distinctly Greek is the push-pull across the palate,” says Bradley Tomlinson of Wine Kiosk in Kalamata, home to one of the country’s most serious selections of Greek wines. “Tomato leaf, green olive, stewed cherries, and allspice seem to compete for dominance.” 

The joy of Xinomavro, pronounced ksi-nO-ma-vro, is in that tension. Once something of a niche curiosity, it’s now making the rounds of wine bars, bistronomy spots, and fine-dining restaurants around the world.

Nebbiolo’s More Affordable Greek Cousin

The wine’s energy, complexity, finesse, and aging potential place it alongside some of the world’s most sought-after red wines, including Burgundian Pinot Noir and Piedmontese Nebbiolo, to which it is often—and quite reasonably—compared. Yet it remains far more affordable. Greece, in this sense, is still a true hunting ground for bargains.

“Xinomavro represents exceptionally good value, even once it reaches export markets,” says François Braouezec, associate at La Cave de Belleville in Paris.

“There is a noticeable increase in sommelier interest, driven by the grape’s appeal to a global preference for freshness, flavor, and terroir clarity over sweetness or heaviness,” says Karakasis.

Producing compelling wine from Xinomavro, however, is far from simple. A demanding variety, its very etymology—derived from the Greek xino (acid) and mavro (black)—points to its naturally high acidity and pronounced tannins, traits that invite comparison with Nebbiolo. As a result, growers and winemakers must keep yields in check and favor gentle extraction.

A late-ripening Xinomavro is also sensitive to disease pressure and performs best in cool, well-ventilated sites. Hillside vineyards at higher elevations, especially when paired with poor soils, help extend the growing season, allowing for full phenolic ripeness without sacrificing freshness.

The Variety That Almost Wasn’t

Xinomavro-based wines are produced across much of northern Greece and bottled under four appellations, but the grape’s heartland lies in the hills of Naoussa. The variety narrowly escaped extinction in the 20th century: Phylloxera, war, poverty, and the widespread replacement of vineyards with more profitable fruit trees nearly wiped out old vines across the region. By 1962, just 50 hectares of vineyards remained in Naoussa.

In 1968, Yiannis Boutaris began replanting the region’s Xinomavro, encouraging his neighbors to move away from blends in favor of varietal bottlings. He played a central role in establishing the first controlled appellations dedicated to the grape. Winemakers such as Apostolos Thymiopoulos and Stergios Thymiopoulos followed. Together, their efforts helped bring Xinomavro onto the international stage.

From Rosés to Traditional Method Sparklers

Rosé lovers should also pay close attention to Xinomavro, as the grape produces some of the category’s most compelling expressions. Expect a complex bouquet of wild berry preserves and ripe apricot skin paired with a soft, creamy texture and real fruit-driven weight—very much a rosé gastronomique. With age, these wines only grow in complexity, revealing notes of rose petals alongside hints of nutmeg and cinnamon.

“In Greece, we are drowning in rosés made from Xinomavro, but no one has flown as close to the sun in burning glory as Apostolos Thymiopoulos with his 2007 and 2013 cuvées of Rosé de Xinomavro,” says Tomlinson. “There are very few left on earth, alas, but if you are lucky enough to come across any these days, there’s still a little life left in them.”

Xinomavro’s versatility extends just as convincingly to sparkling wines made by the traditional method. Here, the grape delivers savory, tightly wound wines with real depth, held in balance by freshness, tension, and structure.“

For sparkling, Amyndeon is the reference point, and Domaine Karanika is the name I highlight most often for traditional method Xinomavro-based wines,” says Karakasis. “Scaperdas Freres is also a very smart, widely liked entry point into sparkling Xinomavro.”

Key Regions to Know for Xinomavro

With elevations spanning roughly 150 to 500 meters and soils ranging from sandy clay to limestone and marl, Naoussa’s mosaic of terroirs allows for markedly different expressions of the grape. Here, the reference style of Xinomavro took shape built around intense perfume, fine tannins, and a distinctly savory profile.

North of Naoussa lies Goumenissa, where limestone-clay and marl-rich soils dominate. Here, Xinomavro is mandatorily blended with Negoska at a minimum of 20%, which deepens color, softens tannins, and bolsters the fruit profile.

Higher in the mountains lies Amyndeon, where vineyards are planted at elevations ranging from roughly 600 to 1,000 meters. Soils here are lighter and predominantly sandy clay. “You tend to find more of the highly anticipated tomato notes and, thanks to the altitude, a little more zing, which is why production leans toward sparkling wines,” says Tomlinson.

The only appellation outside Macedonia where Xinomavro plays a central role is Rapsani, located in northern Thessaly. Vineyards here are planted on complex soils of clay, loam, and limestone at elevations of approximately 400 to 700 meters. Combined with a significant diurnal temperature range, these conditions give rise to wines that are both structured and aromatically complex.

“It is the holy trio of Xinomavro, Stavroto, and Krassato that sets the appellation apart—always blended, always barrel-aged, and always requiring a little patience before pleasure,” says Tomlinson.

How to Pair Xinomavro

Xinomavro feels most at home alongside slow-cooked dishes—like braised meats in tomato-based sauces with herbs and spices—where its acidity and tannins engage with rich, dense textures.

“If it isn’t a dead animal over flame, then I’d pivot to a stifado,” says Tomlinson. “It’s a much-loved, slow-cooked, simple stew made with pearl onions and beef chuck.”

For Karakasis, the ultimate home-run pairing is slow-cooked lamb or goat with tomato and herbs. “The acidity cuts the richness, the tannins latch onto the protein, and the wine’s tomato-and-herb character mirrors the dish rather than fighting it,” he says. “It’s one of those pairings where the wine tastes more Greek with food than without it.”

title: Another Climate-Related Disaster? Winemaker Mental Health
summary:
The financial and physical toll these disasters take on producers is well-documented. Less so is the effect they have on mental wellbeing.

The post Another Climate-Related Disaster? Winemaker Mental Health appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Australian wine producer Matt Fowles spent the first few days of 2026 battling flames that would eventually engulf his livestock, equipment, and storage sheds. The entirety of his vineyards—all 300 acres in Victoria’s Strathbogie Ranges—had already burned in what would be dubbed one of the most catastrophic fires in the state’s history. At least he’d managed to save the family home. Or so he thought. But by the next evening, his house, too, had succumbed to the raging flames, along with his uncle’s home and a guest house on the property. Fowles spent the six hour drive to Melbourne, where his family was sheltering, in a state of complete shock. 

Three months later, the outcome of the fire is finally sinking in.

“The last two weeks have been where the grief side of things has reared its head more than the shock,” the normally gregarious Fowles says quietly, his voice shaking a little. “It’s starting to be a tough patch.”

Since the inception of life on planet Earth, natural disasters have been par for the course. But the last decade has seen an unprecedented rise in climate-related disasters—and wine country has been hit hard. 

The financial and physical toll these disasters take on producers is well documented. Less so is the effect they have on mental wellbeing.

A Rising Toll

“I had something like a nervous breakdown,” Meike Näkel of Weingut Meyer-Näkel recalls of the days and weeks that followed the devastating flood that washed away her winery. 

On July 14th 2021, when the Ahr River in western Germany burst its banks, fifth-generation winemaking sisters Meike and Dörte Näkel were washed from their winery as its walls started to collapse. They clung to a nearby tree for eight hours in the dark listening to cries of their neighbors as winery equipment, cars, even houses, rushed past them. 

That awful night wasn’t the worst of it, though. The real blow came after they were rescued and realized everything was gone. What little energy they had left drained from them. “I was so devastated,” Meike says.

From floods to fires, these catastrophic losses are increasingly common across the globe.

California wine country experienced destructive wildfires six out of the last ten years. Just before the 2023 vintage, Cyclone Gabrielle tore through New Zealand’s North Island wine regions, burying vineyards in river sludge. Four months later, Italy’s Emilia-Romagna region flooded, after historic droughts, causing 2,000 mudslides and total devastation. One year later, in May 2024, 1,200 acres of vines in Brazil’s main wine-producing region of Rio Grande do Sul were destroyed in floods. And, just days after the Victoria wildfires engulfed Fowles’ entire life, South Africa’s Western Cape was set ablaze.

Combine climate change with a slew of other economic and geopolitical challenges currently plaguing the planet’s winemakers: the reality is that  producers today are experiencing stress at levels the generation before them couldn’t have imagined. 

Suicide rates in the U.S. have risen to their highest levels since 1941—and individuals in farming are two to five times as likely to take their own lives. In 2021, at least four French winemakers died by suicide. There has been a steady stream of others since. 

While this paints a grim picture, it’s also true that people are more open and willing to seek mental health care than ever before. Translating that to the majority male, rural landscape of wine-farming—two factors that decrease therapy rates significantly—remains a major challenge.

The Hurdles to Wellness

“It’s been a quiet subject until late, and it’s such an important topic,” says Matthew Spaccarelli, a second-generation wine farmer and producer at Fjord Vineyards and Benmarl Winery in New York’s Hudson Valley. 

He’s exhausted. It’s the third week of April 2026 and he’s been up all night lighting fires in an attempt to mitigate the fast and furious frosts that are becoming increasingly common. Warmer spring temperatures push out vine buds earlier in the season, making them vulnerable when temperatures plummet again. Invasive pests—the latest being the spotted lanternfly—also keep Spaccarelli up at night. Farming a one-time-a-year crop comes with a number of stressors, actually, and they all take a toll.

“They pile up on each other because you’re exhausted when you get home and it’s like, ‘Do I want to find the time [for my own wellbeing], or do I want to drink a beer and put on a football match?’”

American wine producers face another challenge unique to the U.S.: the cost of healthcare, including mental health support.

“Health insurance [in the States] is ridiculous,” says Spaccarelli. “Mine went up 20% this year.”

Then there’s the issue of time, whether it’s finding a therapist or even just making an hour-long session during harvest and other busy farming seasons. “You’re working eight days a month just to pay for healthcare that you don’t even have time to use,” he adds. “Those are really big hurdles for people in our industry.”

While Americans battle with a broken healthcare system, in France and other parts of Europe, the challenges are more culturally rooted.

“I just don’t think people think about [therapy], it’s still very taboo” says Sarah Hwang, a New Jersey native who has managed Vouvray winery, Domaine Huet, since 2012, after her father purchased the Loire Valley estate in 2003. 

Especially in small towns, like Vouvray, she says people fear judgment over going to therapy. “It’s a cultural thing,” she says. “It’s totally different than in the U.S. where everybody is in therapy all the time.”

Germany faces similar cultural hurdles. 

Julia Bertram, a fifth-generation Ahr Valley winegrower and co-founder of Weingut Bertram-Baltes, doesn’t see mental health having “a place in society,” she says. She and her husband, Benedikt Baltes, also endured devastating losses from the floods. Despite the huge toll on growers in the region, she says, “there’s still a lot of silence surrounding mental illness.”

A Meeting of Minds

German culture may not openly embrace counseling, but both Bertram and Näkel emphasize the impact of community support. 

After the flood, the community came together to spend time talking about rebuilding their lives. For an entire year, Näkel’s mother—along with other older local women who couldn’t manage the heavy lifting of clean up—served breakfast from local food donations to the community from a garage. People would just stop by for a coffee or snack. 

“You could just chat, sip, talk about how the day was,” Näkel says. “Someone might say, ‘It was a very awful day,’ and you’d say, ‘I also had an awful day!’ And you could laugh and have the weight of this terrible time lifted a little because you were handling it together.”

In the Loire, a region plagued by torrential rain and frosts in recent years, Hwang says many wine professionals find stress relief from outdoor activities and hobbies. “And vacation is very important here,” she says. “People go away for a good amount of time and really switch off.”

In Australia, the government is promoting mental health through social media campaigns, presentations, and the web. After the January wildfires, the state organizing body, Wine Victoria, set up an information page that included mental health resources.

“We then communicated directly with those wineries impacted, ensuring they were aware of the mental health services available,” Wine Victoria CEO Stephanie Duboudin says. 

Many producers claimed it helped to reduce anxiety.

In October, 2022, The Wine Grape Council of South Australia (WGCSA) released a podcast series titled “Healthy Minds, Healthy Vines,” to help open conversations around mental health and wellbeing among winegrowers. Currently in its third season, the podcast features a range of guests from producers to mental health professionals, including men’s mental health specialist, Dr. Ian Zajac.

In Australia (and in the U.S., too), men are half as likely to obtain mental health treatment when compared to women, notes the The Flinders University research fellow, whose focus is on suicide prevention. But in Zajac’s clinical psychology work, he often sees rapid improvements in male clients. “It can definitely work,” he says. “But there remains a reluctance in some segments of the population.” 

While cost is a big barrier, stigma is the biggest. Zajac has found that when men hear “from other guys who have had good outcomes, it has helped clients shift their thinking to enable them to enter health care settings.”

An Open Mind

Fowles has inadvertently become one of those guys who has had good outcomes from therapy. While he doesn’t think therapy is the “be all and end all,” but rather “one tool in the arsenal,” the fires have enabled him to see the benefits of opening up about seeking professional help.

“Probably in past times, I wouldn’t have talked about it,” he says. “But I feel there’s a responsibility to my family and to my team to let them know there’s no shame attached to it, to show them that it’s a normal and healthy thing to do” 

For other producers impacted by life-changing natural disasters, like Näkel, the work of rebuilding is what’s helped most. “You have chaos, and in chaos you have energy, and we took that energy and put it in a new direction,” she says. 

Näkel and her sister are moving the winery to higher ground, far from the river. This fresh start also gave them the courage to pursue an organic farming certification in 2022, which they’d previously worried would be too difficult on their steep slopes. “After the flood we realized we were so much more capable,” she says. “We felt like we could face anything.”

Fowles’ wounds from losing so much are far more raw. But he’s been leaning on professional help to rebuild his life and business. He regularly talks to people he trusts, including business advisors, and has reestablished an exercise routine, which he says, “has been massive” for improving his mental health.

He’s also been reminded of what’s important in life. “There are blessings in this, and you have to take those blessings and really run with them,” he says. “It complicates grief, but it gives you hope as well.”

How to Navigate Mental Health Challenges 

May is National Mental Health Awareness Month. This year’s theme, “More Good Days, Together” focuses on community support, fostering connection, and supporting individuals to find individual mental wellness practices that work for them. Below, we asked Zajac for his tips on improving mental health in the wine industry—and beyond.

Beyond therapy, how can those working in the wine industry look after their mental health?

Dr. Ian Zajac: The first thing is to think about your daily protocols. What things are actually helpful (exercise, diet, socialization) and how can you set up a routine that makes these things non-negotiables on a daily basis. 

Decision-making skills are impaired when your mental health is not in a good space. You are more likely to make poor decisions, and having a structured protocol helps to reduce the decision load. Try to incorporate a friend or partner where possible to increase connection and support.

Are there signs that members of the wine industry can watch for in their colleagues and friends that might indicate they’d benefit from seeking professional mental health help?

IZ: One of the major signs that someone is not doing well includes changes in social behavior. For example, someone who is usually quite social could withdraw. This doesn’t necessarily mean not attending social settings; often, they just become less talkative, or hang on the outer of the group, which is unlike them. The next extreme is the complete opposite: they might seem more intense and engaged, but often that is fueled by alcohol, or another substance that helps them to shift how they are feeling. Either way, the person is changing how they show up socially and changes are apparent to those who pay attention. 

Alongside this, we need to be aware of life stressors in the background. In Australia, a leading cause of suicide in middle aged guys is relationship breakdown and separation. After that, we have job changes, financial pressure, legal challenges, and other relationship issues. If you know someone going through any of these things, the important thing to do is ask directly “How are you going navigating the separation?,” for example. Even if they don’t want to talk, knowing that others are aware and checking in can be very powerful.

If you or someone you know is struggling with mental health challenges, emotional distress, substance use problems, or just needs to talk, call or text 988, or chat at 988lifeline.org 24/7. 

Alternatively, The National Suicide Prevention Lifeline can be reached at 1-800-273-TALK (8255), by texting “STRENGTH” to the Crisis Text Line at 741-741, or at suicidepreventionlifeline.org.

title: Tannat Deserves a Spot on Your Table
summary:
From the age-worthy bottles of Madiran to experimental New World styles, Tannat’s diversity spans a wide variety of pairings and occasions.

The post Tannat Deserves a Spot on Your Table appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Tannat is most often associated with the full-bodied, brambly wines of the Madiran appellation of Southwest France and the remarkably refined versions from Uruguay. However, single-varietal bottlings of the powerhouse grape have been gaining traction across the globe in regions like Oregon’s Applegate Valley and California’s Central Coast.

With their intense flavor, robust tannins, and ability to shine alongside hearty, flavorful dishes, these wines are worthy of more attention than they receive.

Tannat is a “bold, powerful variety that delves deep into the black fruit side of the flavor spectrum,” says Wine Enthusiast Writer-at-Large Matt Kettmann, who reviews wines from California and France. He notes that while these wines often carry significant weight, they don’t always necessarily overwhelm with structure.

California-grown Tannats, in particular, tend to be approachable soon after release, making them an exciting option for drinkers looking for intensity without a long wait. But Tannat styles vary widely depending on where they’re grown and made.

A Study in Terroir and Technique

In recent years, Uruguay has become a standard-bearer for modern iterations of the grape, producing wines that are both powerful and surprisingly refined. While France’s Madiran Tannat—which traditionally was used in blends—leans toward a more structured, deeply tannic, rustic style, Uruguayan versions often exhibit a softer edge, making them enjoyable both young and with age.

This is the result of better vineyard management and changes in winemaking techniques, such as a more judicious use of oak, which had led to smoother, fresher expressions of the grape. These well-balanced wines often offer “floral and fruit-forward character and elegant tannins,” says Jesica Vargas, a Wine Enthusiast Writer-at-Large who reviews wines from Latin America and Africa.

Bold Wines for Boldly-Flavored Fare

As for pairings, both Kettmann and Vargas agree—Tannat demands dishes with intensity of their own.

“Tannat is a great barbecue and stew wine,” Kettmann explains. “It requires food with big flavors, and Tannat works very well with more rustic rather than refined foods.”

Similarly, Uruguayan Tannats play well with grilled meats and stews. However, Vargas notes, their versatility is worth exploring, as these wines also shine with stuffed pastas like braised veal ravioli.

While versatility and food-friendliness are reasons enough to try Tannat, perhaps the most compelling aspect to these wines is their nascent development in regions with no historic ties to the grape.

In Paso Robles and Oregon, for example, winemakers are still exploring what their unique climates can do for Tannat. “There is no clear formula for what it should be, which makes it an exciting grape to pursue,” says Kettmann. “If you start soon, you’ll be able to watch its evolution in real time.”

As these regions continue experimenting with different vineyard sites, soils, and aging methods, the diversity of Tannat expressions will keep expanding. Whether winemakers lean into the grape’s robust tannins or aim for a fresher, softer profile, the journey of Tannat, especially in the New World, is still unfolding.

From the structured, age-worthy bottles of Madiran to the polished yet powerful expressions from Uruguay and the experimental New World styles emerging in the U.S., the bottles highlighted below showcase why Tannat deserves a spot on your table.

Wine Enthusiast Recommends:

California

Écluse 2023 Lock Vineyard Tannat (Paso Robles)

This single-vineyard Tannat bottling is potent on the nose with black cherry and French toast aromas. The palate’s richness is balanced by a tense texture, as flavors of black cherry, cola and root beer ride a prickly acidity through the finish. 92 Points — Matt Kettmann

$64 Écluse

Ursa Vineyards 2020 Ferrel Tannat (El Dorado County)

The notes on the aromas are black currant, blueberry, allspice, citrus, and violets on the nose. The palate is dark plum, blackberry, vanilla extract, orange brulee, clove, and grippy tannins on the finish. Decant and enjoy. 91 Points — Tonya Pitts

$36 Bending Branch Winery

Sobon Estate 2022 ReZerve® Tannat (Amador County)

This guilty pleasure is unusual because the varietal is usually used as a blending grape to add more structure. At the same time, these aromas are exuberant, lush with notes of dark ripe berries, pie crust, baking spice, and toasty vanilla on the nose. The flavors repeat, but they are balanced, youthful, grippy tannins and mouthwatering acidity to cut through your favorite braised dishes. Pair with Braised Beef Cheeks, Watercress, Potatoes, Dijon Mustard, Braising Jus. 92 Points — Tonya Pitts

$28 Sobon Wine Company, LLC

France

Château Peyros 2022 Greenwich 43N Tannat (Madiran)

This is a dense and powerful wine with rich black fruits, a black-coffee aroma and dark tannins. It has concentration and bold berry flavors. Named after the situation of the vineyard on the intersection of the Greenwich 0 degree longitude and the 43 degree latitude. The wine is set for aging, wait until 2027. Watch the alcohol. Cellar Selection. 94 Points — Roger Voss

$43 Total Wine & More

Château Laffitte Teston 2020 Vieilles Vignes Tannat (Madiran)

Wood-aged, old-vine Tannat gives a powerful wine. Vanilla and licorice aromas, rich tannins and dense black fruits all reinforce the wine’s solid texture. With its dry core still intact, the wine needs much more time. Drink from 2027. Editor’s Choice. 93 Points — R.V.

$27 Chain Bridge Cellars

Domaine Loaugué 2022 Marty Tannat (Madiran)

A powerful wine, dense with black fruits and rich tannins, this is full of aging potential. Its licorice aroma, chocolate and ripe black fruit flavors are impressive, showing the concentration from Tannat. Drink from 2027. Cellar Selection. 94 Points — R.V.

$29 Mission Liquor

Oregon

Day 2021 TNT Tannat (Applegate Valley)

100% Tannat at 12% ABV is a beautiful thing. But don’t worry, this wine made with fruit from Layne Vineyard is sporting major flavor and the muscular tannins to back it up. Dynamite aromas of dark plums, boysenberry gelato and violets are accented by mint and chalk. Tart blackberries, sage and Irish breakfast tea flavors fill the mouth and rough up the roof of the mouth. 93 Points — Michael Alberty

$36 Day Wines

Uruguay

Familia Deicas 2023 Atlántico Sur Tannat (Uruguay)

Enchanting aromas of cherries lead the way on this well-balanced Tannat. A medley of berries is followed by light notes of sage on the palate, adding a herbal touch to the finish. Best Buy. 90 Points — Jesica Vargas

$64 My Cava

Bodega Garzón 2023 Reserva Tannat (Maldonado)

2026 The California Issue This year’s California Issue is especially poignant as the Golden State celebrates the 50th anniversary of the Judgment of Paris in the midst of growing existential challenges. We talked to wine industry veterans about the best path forward, hunted for truffles in Paso Robles, paired the best tacos in Los Angeles

The post California Issue – 2026 appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
2026

The California Issue

This year’s California Issue is especially poignant as the Golden State celebrates the 50th anniversary of the Judgment of Paris in the midst of growing existential challenges. We talked to wine industry veterans about the best path forward, hunted for truffles in Paso Robles, paired the best tacos in Los Angeles with California wines, and went down to Valle de Guadalupe to investigate the unsolved mystery of Mexican Nebbiolo.

LA’s Best Wine Bars Are Hidden in Plain Sight

Among the celebrity haunts, old-school classics, and Michelin-star chasers, an unpretentious wine scene is the real reason to love LA.

Read More

Letter from the Publisher

We’re Down With J.O.P (Yeah, You Know Me)

California’s audacity lives on 50 years later, and we need it now more than ever.

In 1976, California was a pip-squeak kid poking the bear of Bordeaux. The sheer audacity it took to sit down at that table and blind taste against the French on their home turf is what we should be drawing inspiration from today. Not the victory. The nerve.

So here we are, at the 50th anniversary of the Judgment of Paris, and I keep asking myself: What are we still trying to prove?

Read More

The real fight isn’t France versus California anymore. Our enemy is bigger and, frankly, scarier. It’s a growing lack of engagement, a culture drifting away from the table, and a generation that hasn’t yet discovered what we already know: Wine is often the connective tissue of social joy.

The 60 Minutes segment “The French Paradox” isn’t coming to the rescue. We never needed France to validate us anyway; we proved that in ’76, and we’ve been proving it every vintage since. California hasn’t just established itself on the world stage; it has set new standards and become an icon entirely on its own terms.

But the next 50 years won’t be won by defending what we’ve built. Today, making great wine is permission to play, not a guarantee you’ll win. California winemakers will succeed by doing what the state has always done best—taking risks, asking questions nobody else is asking, planting grapes nobody thought would work, and ripping out the ones that no longer serve us. The producers I’m most excited about right now are the ones willing to lose it all to find something new.

California’s land is an embarrassment of riches that no other wine region on Earth can replicate. Staggering coastline. Fruit-powering sunshine. A grower culture that is intrinsically down to earth. The Golden State has none of the formality of the Old World, just the authenticity that this moment demands—genuine dialogue, real stories, and a sense of place so vivid it practically speaks for itself.

That spirit lives in these pages. Every story in this issue is a story about people risking it, rethinking it, betting on California the way Chateau Montelena and Stag’s Leap bet on themselves in a Paris hotel in May of 1976. Fifty years later, California’s place in wine history is established. But the unsettled part is the most interesting part. Let’s take these stories as inspiration.

title: Winemakers Without Borders: Blurring the Divide Between Alta and Baja California
summary:
The success of Valle de Guadalupe is fueled by vintners who are bridging the Alta-Baja California divide.

The post Winemakers Without Borders: Blurring the Divide Between Alta and Baja California appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
When Tony Viramontes was 18, he left his home in Zacatecas, Mexico, to find work in the United States. He told his mom he’d be back someday.

It took 35 years of climbing Northern California’s corporate ladders, culminating in a VP of Operations role at Jackson Family Wines, but Viramontes is now tending to his homeland. He and his brother, Gerardo Viramontes, also a NorCal wine industry vet, are bringing what they’ve learned in the States to Mexico’s wine regions. And as they’ve done for decades, they continue to help hundreds of workers legally enter the States each year, since labor is in dire demand.

The brothers’ viticultural focus is a 30-acre vineyard in the seaside hills above Valle de Guadalupe in Baja California. “Valle,” as it’s known, is Mexico’s primary wine country, located 70 miles south of the U.S. border and teeming with about 200 estates and five Michelin-starred restaurants.

Rather than settle on the crowded valley floor, the Viramontes brothers are applying their Sonoma Coast know-how to this cooler elevated region, which they’ve been calling Costa de Valle. “I am looking for that Pacific influence,” Tony said on a sunny March morning as we sipped tropical Sauvignon Blanc, savory Petite Sirah rosé, and fresh red blends from their brands West Soul and Juego de Pelota. “I want to farm on the hill and make a different kind of wine.”

Following their typical routine, the brothers, who live in Healdsburg, flew down to San Diego the day before, slept in Tijuana, and drove an hour-plus south that morning to check on their vines. After a lunch of lobster tacos on a bluff overlooking the ocean, they returned to Tijuana that afternoon to deal with workers awaiting visas, and then headed home to Sonoma County. The following week, they’d be in Querétaro, where they manage another vineyard. Crossing the border is their way of life.

“Working between Baja and Alta California has shaped not only how we grow and make wine, but also how we think about continuity, identity, and long-term stewardship in wine regions that are geographically divided yet deeply intertwined,” explained Tony. “Bridging these worlds through vineyards and wine has been central to our work for decades.”

It only took me a few hours in the country to understand that similar bridges span the past, present, and future of Valle de Guadalupe—connections between Mexico and the States, of course, but also Europe, Australia, and South America. This culture of back-and-forth immigration, and the cross-pollination of ideas that it fosters, isn’t just a chapter of the Valle story. It’s the whole book.

“Most of us come from elsewhere,” said Fernando Pérez Castro, a former Mexico City actor who runs the Finca la Carrodilla and La Lomita wineries for his family. “We have people from England, the Netherlands, France, Spain, Italy, all over Mexico. There are a lot of cultures mixed up. You don’t find that in other parts of Mexico.”

This melting-pot environment has given Valle its own rich identity. “We don’t pretend to be Napa or Rioja or Bordeaux,” Pérez Castro said. “We are very proud to be Mexico, and we believe we have everything to offer.”

Homecoming is what brought the Viramonteses back to Valle de Guadalupe, but others came here to find a sense of purpose. Kristin Magnussen was a sandy-haired California girl working restaurant jobs in Santa Barbara when her dad and stepmom bought a small Valle ranch in the early 2000s. They planted the first vines with cuttings from neighbors, harvested grapes with help from volunteer firefighters, and encouraged Kristin to study wine at University of California, Davis.

At first, she declined, but then the scene matured. “The energy was infectious,” said Magnussen, who moved to Valle to run Vinos Lechuza in 2013. “In Santa Barbara, I was just treading water. Let’s give it a go.”

It hasn’t always been easy, especially when her dad died suddenly in 2017, but it’s all working out. “My kids are allowed to be kids,” she said. “We eat well—all of our friends are chefs. We drink well—all of our friends are winemakers. We live such a rich life that when I travel, I want to come home.”

She’s constantly impressed by the freedom that the region’s entrepreneurial spirit allows. “I have the ability to learn and take risks,” she said. “As a female and as an immigrant, that just doesn’t work on paper.”

As a border-straddling gringa, she’s conscious of her responsibility to both sides. “We want to be good ambassadors of Mexico in the United States,” she said, “and we want to be good ambassadors of the United States in Mexico.”

Like Magnussen, Nathan Malagón was born and raised in Alta California, on a ranch near San Juan Capistrano. But his great-grandfather, who was from Guanajuato, had bought land in Valle de Guadalupe in the 1960s. “I was Peter Panning around in my 20s,” said Malagón, who was planning to teach English and surf in Brazil at the time.

Then his dad suggested that Nathan take care of the family ranch in Valle. “This place saved my life,” said Malagón, who rehabbed the 92-year-old vineyard and became entrenched in the community. “It gave me direction.” It also connected him to his wife, winemaker Verónica Santiago, who’d been raised in Ensenada but studied wine in Australia.

They fell in love, launched Mina Penélope in 2014, and now grow Aglianico, Montepulciano, Nebbiolo, Mourvèdre, and Sauvignon Blanc on about eight acres near the coast. While Verónica handles the cellar—a former garage and tool shed under their house—Nathan tends to the vines, ever on the lookout for varieties that can handle this dry region. “The idea here is always: What is more resilient?” he said.

Immigration is what originally ignited Valle de Guadalupe’s agricultural explosion. The wave of Russians who arrived in the early 1900s developed extensive farming operations across 13,000 acres here. Nikolai Rudametkin is a descendant of those settlers. Born in San Diego and raised in Ensenada, he was 16 when his uncle, Fernando Pérez Castro, opened La Lomita and Finca de Carrodilla on their family’s ranches in 2009.

His grandfather paid for Rudametkin to study oenology at UC Davis. “I was an investment,” said Rudametkin, who then worked at Peju and St. Supéry in Napa, Fingers Crossed in Ojai, and Bodega Garzón in Uruguay. He was working in Argentina this spring, and aiming to work a harvest in Champagne this fall.

“That’s how I want to live my life—as an international consultant,” said Rudametkin, who’s proud that his family’s wines were the first in Mexico to be certified organic. “If I become a great winemaker, I’ll just be another great winemaker. But if I become a great viticulturist, I can help everyone become better growers. Then everybody can make better wines.”

That’s been the lifelong mission of Italian-born Camillo Magoni, who arrived in Valle in 1965 and worked for L.A. Cetto winery for nearly half a century before launching his own brand in 2013. “It was like yesterday,” said the beloved Valle pioneer, who introduced stainless steel tanks and Petite Sirah to the region. “It was the best decision of my life.”

His curious spirit never wavered. He now grows a dizzying 121 different varieties across more than 280 acres, which are bottled as Casa Magoni and served beneath a 450-year-old oak tree. His daughter Monica manages operations and his other daughter, Mariela, serves small dishes like sea snail crudo in olive oil and rosemary flowers from the winery’s kitchen.

“We were born in San Diego and raised in Tijuana,” said Mariela, who’s also taught culinary classes in California for 14 years. “But we’re constantly crossing the border.”

Magoni also influenced Tijuana-born winemaker Javier Flores, who works at South Coast Winery in California’s Temecula Valley. Flores’ father worked for Camillo, and so did Javier when his dad got sick with cancer and later died. The younger Flores tried to quit school and work in the cellar multiple times, but Magoni wouldn’t let him and even paid for his education at times.

“I was hiding. I wanted to work,” said Flores, who graduated from Instituto Tecnológico de Tijuana and took a job in winemaking in Temecula in 1989. “[Magoni] said, ‘You’re going! If you don’t leave, I’m gonna kick you out.” He still relies on Magoni for viticultural advice, explaining, “He’s always a phone call away.”

If Magoni epitomizes Valle’s past, Oscar Mancillas is a glimpse into its future. Raised among winemaking families in Ensenada, Mancillas worked in the cellars of Valle legend Hugo d’Acosta at a young age, then studied wine in Spain. For two years, he lived in the Roussillon region of France, producing a brand called Domaine Nada, but then came back to Valle, encouraged by the folks at Bichi in nearby Tecate.

“I had to plant my roots somewhere,” said Mancillas, who launched his brand, Radicantes, in 2023. He’s now running the winery La Escuelita, or “little school,” a nod to the building’s legacy as a classroom of sorts for many Valle winemakers, and continues to consult for estates in the Ramona Valley east of San Diego.

While many Valle wines stick to classic styles, Radicantes bottlings venture into nattier expressions of both freshness and funk. Mancillas is expanding his operations into his family’s vineyard, where he’s planting Grenache Gris, Mission, Colombard, and Sumoll.

This border-crossing culture extends into the region’s cuisine as well. About 45 minutes north of the border in Oceanside, Roberto Alcocer runs the Michelin-starred restaurant Valle. Raised in Ensenada, Alcocer learned English by helping his parents sell trinkets to cruise ship tourists and at weekend swap meets in San Diego, when crossing the border “was so easy.”

He spent years in France learning to cook, eventually landing a high-paying job preparing food for energy-company executives. “I was making good money, but I was seeing my friends become renowned chefs in my country—maybe they weren’t making money, but they were happy and successful,” he said. “Valle was blooming.”

To get in on the action, he opened Malva on the Mina Penélope property in 2014, quickly becoming a must-stop for any Valle visitor. The restaurant closed earlier this year, so he’s been on the search for his next spot, where he’ll be hunting a Michelin star.

“The next Malva is not going to be a palapa,” said Alcocoer. “The next Malva has to show the love and respect I have for Valle. We’ll see another blooming, and I want to be there for it.”

Where to Stay

You can find every type of lodging under the sun in Valle de Guadalupe, from affordable glamping options and small bed-and- breakfasts to fully decked-out resorts.

A luxury leader is El Cielo Resort, developed by a hospitality veteran whose wife is one of Mexico’s top sommeliers. Two-story suites overlook a central lake, and there’s a winery, multiple restaurants, and creative visitor experiences.

Modern, desert-influenced design takes center stage at many hotels in Valle. Bruma Wine Resort is an excellent example, offering single bedrooms all the way up to an entire villa that brings the nature vibes inside.

Centrally located and affordably priced is Surya Hotel & Winery, where the decor recalls the ranchos of old Mexico.

Where to Eat

With 25 Michelin-recommended restaurants in a small area—including five with Michelin stars—Valle de Guadalupe offers a concentrated collection of culinary wonders.

Animalon is considered one of the best restaurants in all of Latin America, while Lunario—owned by the family behind La Lomita and Finca de Carrodilla wineries— does farm-to-table cooking Ensenada style.

For breakfast, everyone recommends La Cocina De Doña Esthela, where the hardest decision is whether to order the corn pancakes or the birria de res, a beefy stew that’s only available on weekends. Get both.

Live-fire cooking dominates the scene, and the pit is certainly the star at Primitivo, a rustic outdoor eatery that shifts its menus based on what meats are available. For a casual wine lunch, try Bodegas Magoni’s curated boards, which showcase local cheeses and meats.

Every resort includes an on-site restaurant, and the most lauded one is Fauna, which is at the Bruma Wine Resort. For a creative twist that blends flavors of Baja with those of the Yucatan, try Latitude 32 at El Cielo.
