title: Australia’s Most Coveted Wine Has No Website, Tasting Room, or Marketing
summary:
On the Wine Enthusiast Podcast, we chat with WE Australia reviewer Christina Pickard on the myth and magic surrounding Wendouree.

The post Australia’s Most Coveted Wine Has No Website, Tasting Room, or Marketing appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
My last trip to Australia was vastly different from my first. Two decades ago, I landed in Melbourne as a 20-year-old backpacker with a short-term work visa and a penchant for whatever booze was cheapest. When we started to hit the dregs of our bag-in-box wine, my friends and I would blow it up to get every last drop (and, sometimes, even reuse it as a camping pillow.)

In early November, I got invited on a South Australia press trip and decided to fly over a bit early to celebrate one of said friend’s 40th birthdays in Perth. Our late nights eating potatoes and sausages (“snags”) were replaced with early dinners of grilled lobster and hanger steaks with chimichurri made by her husband with her five-year-old son. At the party with her extended family and longtime friends, the cases of Victoria Bitters we would’ve drank 20 years ago were replaced with $100 bottles of Victorian Pinot Noir.

The conversation changed from “how to get the most beer or wine for your dollar” to “how to find the best Australia has to offer.” And when I mentioned I was heading to the Clare Valley, one of the friends lit up, “Have you tried Wendouree?”

That was the first time I had heard of the revered, historic winery. It was far from the last. From Margaret River to almost every restaurant, pub, and cellar door through the Clare, the cult classic was mentioned time and time again. It became clear to me that it’s become one of the most revered wines in Australia. Fortunately, I was able to snag a bottle of the 2022 Malbec—a rare feat made possible by generous Publican Paul Longbottom, the now-former owner of SevenHill Hotel.

It was wonderful: dark-fruited and herbal, supple and not-too-tannic. Still wanting to understand why Wendouree is such a big deal, I reached out to Wine Enthusiast Australia Reviewer Christina Pickard to get the scoop. Unsurprisingly, she had a lot to say.

Turns out, after a 1995 article on the 100-year-anniversary of the winery, Aussie wine lovers went berserk. Pickard tells me, the owners got so many calls from potential customers they “pulled the phone cord out of the wall, and the phone company actually threatened her with a fine.”

I was even more intrigued—and, of course, had questions—so I decided to ask them on air. In this episode of the Wine Enthusiast Podcast, Pickard and I go deep into Wendouree, its cult following, and what it says about Australian wine and culture in general. Read on for details.

On Wendouree’s rich history

Christina Pickard: “It was founded originally in 1895 by a guy called Alfred Percy Birks, or as he’s known A.P. Birks. It was passed on to his son in 1917, after he retired. Roly Birks sort of continued on in the same way, selling these wines by horse and cart, by the cask to Melbourne and to other cities in Australia. 

It was purchased by Tony and Lita Brady in the 1970s and that’s when we kind of fast-forward to what we know as Wendouree today. There have been vines planted throughout the 20th century, but there are still some of the old 1890s planting, so some serious old vine material there.

A lot of what’s there is original: the original building, the equipment are all still there. So visiting is really like a step back into time in a lot of ways. They have done very little in terms of changing much. The label is exactly the same as it has been with the big, like A.P. Birks on the front. It’s a very old-fashioned label, like the winery itself. 

The Brady’s basically went on to create—unwillingly and unknowingly—this cult brand.”

On why Wendouree is such a big deal

CP: “For a lot of Aussies, Wendouree is a very well-known name, even though most haven’t actually tasted the wines. It’s in South Australia, in the Clare Valley, which is known for Riesling. Wendouree, however, only produces reds, so already they’re kind of outside the typical canon of that region.

In 1995 they had a journalist write an article for the Financial Review in Australia about the winery centennial, and it all just kind of went apeshit after that. Lita told me that she actually pulled the phone cord out of the wall, and the phone company actually threatened her with a fine. They had so many people calling wanting wine, like wanting to get connected. It basically has not really slowed down the demand, since from ‘95 onwards they have sold out of every vintage every year. They closed to the public after ‘95 because they just found that people were coming to the winery, buying the wines, and then reselling it, and they have always really been big on wanting people to enjoy the wines themselves.

You can find Wendouree in secondary markets, auctions, things like that, but they try to not have that happen.

Part of the myth and the magic around Wendouree is that there’s no tasting room. There’s no email. There’s no websites. There’s no distribution network. There’s no marketing. There’s no sales people. Every winter they hand write out mailings to a few thousand customers and they send it out via snail mail. You then have to fill out the letter yourself and send it back to them via snail mail. And that’s how they’ve stayed, and they have no plans to change.”

How Wendouree compares to American cult wines (like Screaming Eagle)

CP: “Wendouree definitely would be classified as a cult wine, but in some ways I kind of hate what that term has become … because it feels very commoditized. Sort of this thing that is just for the elite. And while it is there, it has become so mythologized because of its scarcity. 

Like Screaming Eagle, you have to wait over a decade to get on their mailing list. I would say probably a lot longer, especially for Wendouree. Screaming Eagle also doesn’t have a tasting room. It doesn’t have tours. It doesn’t have an email address, although they do have a website. It’s also a brand that is quite deliberately shrouded in mystery, but that’s really where the similarities end, even though they’re both cult brands. 

Screaming Eagle has a very different history and a very different setup currently. Unlike Wendouree, it sells for thousands of dollars, like straight off the gates. So they are very untouchable to your average person.

With Wendouree, if you can get on that list, 75 bucks is not that bad. A lot of people can afford that. But, Screaming Eagle,even if you were to get on the mailing list, it’s pretty hard for most people to be able to afford wine that costs thousands of dollars. So those prices, to me, have just gotten jaw-dropping. I was reading about a 1992 vintage that sold at a Napa auction for $500,000 for one bottle, so that to me is kind of crazy. Wendouree doesn’t even touch that. There’s a lot more romanticism. To me, they’re a cult winery in the way that I would kind of want all cult wineries to be.”

On the profile of Clare Valley wines in general

CP: “The Clare Valley is arguably the most famous region for Riesling. It’s a historic region about two hours north of Adelaide. You have five different sub regions, 11 different soil types with super old 200 million year old soil—I can’t even wrap my head around how old that dirt is. It is kind of a continental climate, but it still gets some of those sea breezes, especially on clear days. It really does still get these huge diurnal shifts, so you get these really warm days, but then these cool nights, and that’s where Clare Valley is really known for that acidity retention, for that brightness of acidity, in their styles of Rieslings. It can sometimes be a little bit intense… bone dry, high acid, and very racy, limey, like white floral character. And they’re wines that can age forever. They’re like bulletproof. I mean, I’ve tried them decades old, and they still are super lively and full of life, and that is still the classic style in Clare. But it is changing, and now you have some young producers who are completely changing the style, and really modernizing and innovating.

Like Wendouree, where you have less extraction, softer tannins, and really get a beautiful silky elegance, light- to midweight-style in the reds, it’s been awesome to see where younger producers are taking their reds. With the whites, too, more are working with oak or lees, just making things more textural, a little bit more in that German style. They actually have a collaboration with Erni Loosen, the famous German winemaker, to make a really German-style of Riesling. They have a solera Riesling that they’re playing with as well, which is like multi-vintage, five different vintages. So, I think they’re emblematic of what this sort of new wave of Clare Valley and Australian producers are doing, which is taking these traditional varieties and just playing around, modernizing them, farming better, you know.”

On the authenticity and deep history of Australian wine

CP: “Australia also has capitalism run rampant in its wine industry. We certainly know that from some of the huge brands that have come out. Big, big multinational companies started in Australia over the years. But it does feel like there’s a particular authenticity around a lot of these smaller brands, and there’s so much exciting stuff happening in Australia at the moment.

The most exciting corners of Aussie wines, the wines I want to drink, the wines I want to write about and share, either come from those younger producers who are innovative and pushing the boundaries and just making some really cool wines, or historic brands where they’ve just been making wine the same way for so long, where walking into their wineries is really like a step back in time.

There are surprisingly quite a few of them in Oz. I think a lot of Americans are surprised by just how historic the Australian wine industry is, and in a lot of ways, actually more so than in the States. They have histories going back to the early 1800s and vines in the ground from the early 1800s in soil that predates the dinosaurs. There is is some seriously old stuff happening in Oz that even to call it a New World country kind of makes me laugh.”

The Guest

Christina Pickard is currently a writer-at-large for Wine Enthusiast, covering the wines of Australia, New Zealand, New York, and Great Britain. She tastes and reviews over 1,500 wines per year from these regions. She has also written for publications around the globe including Decanter, Gourmet Traveller Wine, James Halliday’s Wine Companion, Afar, and Food & Wine. As a presenter and educator, Christina was the co-host of one of wine’s first podcasts, The Crush. She was a regular television and live presenter in the U.K. and the founder of School of Wine in Western Australia.

Sara Ventiera 

Hello, and welcome to the Wine Enthusiast Podcast, your serving of drinks, culture, and the people who drive it. I’m Sara Ventiera, digital editorial director here at Wine Enthusiast. When you think of Australian wine, the first thing that comes to mind is a big Shiraz with some sort of cute animal in the bottle on the higher end, maybe Penfolds. According to Wine Australia, in the 12 months that ended in March 2025 Australia exported 106 million liters of wine to the United States, down 21 million liters from the previous year. The US is its second largest market by volume, just behind the United Kingdom, but ranks third for volume, behind both mainland China and the UK. It’s safe to say that most Americans are more familiar with the country’s bulk wines than the good stuff, but as Wine Enthusiast Aussie wine reviewer and evangelist Christina Pickard consistently shares, the land down under boasts an incredible wine scene with tons of high-end bottlings that can go toe to toe with California and the rest of the world. Ask an Australian wine aficionado about bucket list bottles, and one name is sure to come up: Wendoree. On a recent trip to Western and South Australia, I can’t even tell you how many times I was asked if I had tried the Clare Valley cult producer. Unlike most wine lovers, though, I was fortunate enough to get a taste, but as soon as I came home, I sent Pickard an email to ask – what’s with the obsession? The wine is good, obviously, but it turns out the uniquely Australian winery has a lot to say about the country’s wine culture and culture in general. So, for this week’s episode of The Wine Enthusiast Podcast, I decided to chat with Pickard to discuss the highly elusive producer, and how it relates to the broader picture. Listen to learn more.

Yeah, and just not as commoditized, you know. It’s just, it feels a little bit more authentic, more romantic. I mean, that’s not to say Australia doesn’t, as we know, have big companies. I mean, Australia also has capitalism run rampant in a lot of, a lot of ways too, and its wine industry. We certainly know that from some of the huge brands that have come out, big, big multinational companies, you know, started in Australia over the years, but it does feel like there’s a, you know, there’s a particular authenticity around a lot of these smaller brands, and there’s so much exciting stuff happening in Australia at the moment, and the stories to me, the most exciting corners of Aussie wines, the wines I want to drink, the wines I want to write about and share, either – this is maybe oversimplifying it – but either come from those historic brands where they’ve just been making wine the same way for so long, walking into their wineries is really like a step back in time, and there are surprisingly quite a few of them in Oz. I think a lot of Americans are surprised by just how historic the Australian wine industry is, and in some ways, you know, in a lot of ways, actually more so than in the States. They have histories going back to the early 1800s and vines in the ground from the early 1800s in soil that predates the dinosaurs, like I mean, there is like there is some seriously old stuff happening in Oz that even to call it a new world country kind of makes me laugh, but so there’s this incredible side of things from those historic wineries that have just been around forever, and then I think Oz, on the flip side of that, also has one of the most exciting kind of young-gun scenes of like younger producers who are innovative and pushing the boundaries and just making some really cool wines and really forward thinking, those two kind of corners, and then there’s so much more in between as well that unfortunately a lot of that doesn’t get here to the States, some of it does, a lot of it doesn’t, but to me it’s really emblematic of the most exciting parts of Australian wine.

SV

What are some of the innovations that are happening in Australia that, like, make Australian wine exciting at the moment?

CP

So many, and it’s frustrating, because you know, on the surface, when you just kind of dig into the data and read the news reports, there’s a lot of challenges in Australian wine, and that’s kind of for a whole other podcast, the many challenges, but a lot of those are the same challenges that are being faced by producers all over the world, like geopolitical, economic, climate change-related problems that are certainly there, but then below all of those kind of negative headlines is I think Australian wine is the most exciting it’s ever been. It’s in… it’s found this middle ground where we had these a lot of these kind of big name big squeaky clean wines coming from all those big names that we know, those critter wines that were that did kind of revolutionize in a way that’s kind of every man’s wine, you know, and being able to just drink wine on a weeknight because it was cheap and cheerful, and there was kind of that phase into the like big bold Shiraz that really were the ones winning all the Parker points, and then then that kind of pendulum swung into the natural wine scene in Australia, where Oz was really at the forefront, even before America was with natural wine, and they went hugely, that that scene was hugely influential on Australian wine as a whole, and now the pendulum has kind of is swinging and has swung back into the center, where you now still have a lot of really outside the box wines, but they’re a little cleaner, they’re a little bit, they’re kind of falling into a little bit more of a classic spectrum style wise, but you know, kind of picking and choosing the things that worked and how they haven’t worked over the last several decades, and so I think that so many Aussie wines are in that real sweet spot between those two, you know. Australia has the most, some of the most innovative climate change research in the world, with the Australian Wine Research Institute, AWRI. It has this Young Gun of wines award that I recommend for any wine geeks that do want to follow, like kind of the cutting edge of wine, follow the Young Gun of Wine awards, because they always are like, they tell me, ‘okay, who should I keep my eye on, like, who’s, you know, doing cool stuff right now, and really innovating’, you know, they’re planting drought-resistant varieties in places like the Riverland, where traditionally they were growing Shiraz, like those are being planted now, because they’re, they work, you know, for a changing environment and changing climate, but they’re also able to produce more of this, like midweight, lighter style wines, so much, I mean, there’s there’s over 160 varieties planted in Australia, like there’s it’s a really diverse wine making country. There’s 65 different GIs, geographical indications, the country’s roughly the same size as the states, so you know it’s really hard to lump anywhere in together. So even just talking about, like, Margaret River, Chardonnay on the west side is just a completely different conversation than talking about, you know, Hunter Valley Semillon on the far on the other side of the country, it’s like talking about Californian wines versus New York wines, there’s that much variation and diversity, but it’s a, it’s a really exciting, exciting country to be able to cover for the magazine.

SV

Yeah, I mean, while also having the context of living there, I’m sure there’s like an extra sort of appreciation or ties to it.

CP

Yeah, for sure. I feel very loyal to it. I’m married to an Aussie, and I have an Aussie dog and an Aussie daughter, and so I feel, and I’m very fortunate to be able to go back once a really once a year since we moved back here 10 years ago. So I’ve really tried to keep up as much as I can. It’s a big country, so there’s.. I’m constantly feeling like, oh no, I need to get back to this place because I’m really overdue a visit here, really overdue a visit here, because it’s just like impossible to get everywhere that you know very frequently, but I do try to keep up, and, and I really, I really wish that Oz was more Aussie wines were more drunk in the states, and that’s also another conversation for another podcast of why that is, why it’s been such an uphill battle, but they definitely deserve to be drunk more, and there’s some really fantastic stuff coming from Down Under, and worth putting on your radars.

SV

So, you mentioned some of the challenges in Australian wine at the moment. We had spoken previously, and you said that there were some, like, economic and, you know, obvious climate challenges. Could you tell us a bit more about what is going on in Australian wine in general? 

CP

Sure, I’ll try to be brief, because obviously that’s a very big, big subject. I mean, Australia does have a history, again, like a lot of other wine regions, there are certain wine-making countries, they’re certainly not alone of this kind of boom and bust, particularly can be a new world problem. Although now you know, as we’re seeing in France, it’s also an old world problem too, but this sort of supply and demand, you know, problem, I mean, where they end up, you know, doing really well, like for example, when Yellowtail in the early 2000s in America really hit the scene here, and like then all of a sudden Australian wine went crazy. I mean, the figures, when you look at the figures of sales growth, the speed in which Australian wine grew in America in those from like 2004 to like 2008 or so is just crazy. So, of course, there was this rush of plantings, and everybody thought, well, that’s it. We captured the American market, everybody was planting vines, wine production just ramped up hugely. And then, for a number of reasons, that crashed in the late, like the late aughts, so around like 2009 2010 it was just a spectacular crash. There’s some really interesting articles from people like Jancis Robinson talking about how rapidly they fell out of favor, and you know, some of that was their own fault, and I think a lot of that was also just market conditions, and due to the American markets falling too. It was, it was a number of things, it was complex, but they, you know, then found themselves in a wine glut with too much wine, because then they had all these vines in the ground, and of course, wine, it’s a one crop a year deal, you know, you get one one crop per year, and it also takes about five years for when you plant from when you plant a vineyard for fruit to even come onto a vine. So the industry of wine moves much slower, like to make to grow and make wine moves at a much slower pace than the economy, or then trends, you know, move because trends people can turn on and off much quicker to a variety or style or country or region than the actual industry can keep up with, so that’s always been a challenge. Not just for Aussie wine, but wines all over, and we are currently in one of those, one of those – I hate to say bust, like boom and bust, but in one of those downturns at the moment, where there is just too much wine for what the market can handle, and then that creates just a really tough situation for growers, of course, it’s often the growers who are the ones that suffer the most at the hands of these, you know, bigger corporate companies who aren’t just aren’t able to absorb that fruit anymore, they’re not buying it, the price of fruit goes way down.

So I guess that’s all touching on the economic side that has been challenging. On the climate change side, you know, Australia, it’s no mystery has has had some really tough vintages again, emphasizing that Australia is a very big country, so what’s happening in one region might be totally different from what’s happening in another region, but you know, just earlier this year there were some terrible bushfires up in some of the mountain ranges in Victoria that were just devastating. The Hunter Valley, for example, this year, after the wettest winter they’d had in 60 years, went into one of their hottest, driest summers, and had sort of fast and furious pick there. There’s regions in the Riverland that have reached like 121 degrees over the summer, so they’ve got some extremes the way that we’re seeing all over the world, right, extreme heat, but then it’s not been all hot all the time. There was the between 2021-2023 brought cooler, wetter weather, La Nina influence to South Australia and Victoria, and then flooding became an issue, and there’s places where frost, like in the Clare Valley, is a perfect example of where frost is increasingly becoming an issue, so these again are not unique to Australia. They are happening literally all over the world. 

There is no corner of the wine world that has not been touched by climate change in some regard or another, but I would venture to say Australia has had it quite tough in recent years. But on the flip side of that, as I mentioned, with the AWRI, the Australian Wine Research Institute, they also, because of that, have some of the most innovative research and technology, and they’re doing some really amazing stuff on the viticulture side to try and mitigate and adapt to this changing climate, so, and also, you know, because of Australia’s just economically speaking again, because of this boom and bust history that they have, they’re pretty resilient. I think whenever they have tough times, they emerge better as a region, you know. So, yes, there are bound to be some casualties, there are going to be wineries that shutter, they are going to be vineyards that are ripped up, which is, of course, terrible and a tragedy, but also what my hope is that despite all of those challenges, they will emerge with an even more resilient wine scene, with even better wines from grapes being grown from regions that should be growing them in the soil, they should be growing them in the climate, they should be growing up and growing them in a sustainable way. In general, I think Australia is really leaders on the viticulture front, leaders on the labor labor front … So I hope that that will just continue, and that out of, as they say, out of these challenges, they will emerge even better than before.

SV

Amazing. Well, one of the things that you just mentioned is Clare Valley and the frost. I think when many people think of Australia, they don’t think of frost or cold in general. I lived there for a year in my early 20s. I lived in Melbourne, so you know, I was used to cold and rain and gray, but I did not know much about Clare Valley or Clare Valley wines. I tend to be a big fan of, like, cooler climate wines, lighter reds, and love Rieslings. So, I was very pleasantly surprised. Can you tell us a little bit more about the Clare Valley in general, and the wines they’re known for, you know, the reputation of the wines in Australia and throughout the world.

CP

Yeah, for sure. I mean, I think our palates are probably similar. I also, you know, at the end of the day, what I pour in my glass usually is also something fresh and cool climate and high acid, and I think that a lot of folks don’t realize what how prominent Riesling is down under, and what an important and historic grape variety it is in Australia. It has a long history there, the Eden Valley, and which is, which is not far away, which is about an hour away, just just north of Barat, just neighboring Barossa really is kind of all all part of the Barossa Valley in a lot of ways, but so Eden Valley and Clare Valley being the two most famous Riesling producers, but you find Riesling in Great Southern or in Western Australia, and all over pockets, as I mentioned, bests in in the Grampians, like you find it, you find it all over Victoria in these cool, cool pockets of of Australia and Tasmania as well. But the Clare Valley is arguably the most famous region for Riesling, and it’s a historic region about two hours north of Adelaide, so you have sub-regions, five different sub-regions, I think there’s Watervale, Polish Hill … 11 different soil types, you know, the super old 200-million-year-old soil, can’t even wrap my head around how old that dirt is. And it is kind of a continental climate, but it’s only like under 40 miles from the sea, so that seems far away, but it still gets some of those sea breezes, especially on clear days, it really does still get a bit of maritime influence, these huge diurnal shifts. So you get these really warm days, but then these cool nights, and that’s where Claire Valley is really known for that acidity retention, for that brightness of acidity, and in their styles of Rieslings, it can, you know, sometimes be a little bit intense. When I first moved there and started tasting Clare’s Rieslings, I was like, whoa, these can strip the enamel off your teeth. The typical Clare style of Riesling is bone dry, high acid, and very racy, limey, like white floral character. And they’re wines that can age forever. They’re like bulletproof. I mean, I’ve tried them decades-old, and they still are super lively and full of life, and that is still the classic style in Clare, but it is changing, and now you have, like, the next, you have some young producers, like Andrew Kenny, for example, of Kenny Wines, is coming in, and he’s kind of shaking things up. Jim Barry Wines is probably one of the best-known, after Grosset, I would say the best-known producer in the Clare, and they’re on their third generation, where you have the two sons coming up, and they’re completely changing the style, and really modernizing and innovating, and doing like what we were talking about with Wendouree, where you know, less extraction, softer tannins, and really get getting this like beautiful silky elegance, like midweight, light to midweight style in the reds. That’s really been awesome to see where they’re taking their reds, but with the whites too, just working more with oak or with leaves, or just making things more textural, a little bit more in that German style. They actually have a collaboration with Ernie Lussen, the famous German winemaker, to make a really German style of Riesling. They have a Solera Riesling that they’re playing with as well, which is like multi vintage, five different vintages. So I think they’re emblematic of what this sort of new wave of Clare Valley and Australian producers are doing, which is like taking these traditional varieties and, and just playing around, modernizing them, farming better, you know. There’s a lot like Grosset and his wife, Jeffrey Grosset, and his wife Stephanie, who has Mount Horrocks. They’re both biodynamic now. I know the Barrys have gone, I don’t think they’re certified organic, but they’re working that way. They’ve gotten rid of herbicides, so many producers.

When I was there recently, they were like, we’re not using herbicides anymore, we’re using cover crops, so less and less people are using glyphosate. Thank the Lord, and more and more people just farming better, farming sustainably, working with what they have in the ground, and replanting when they, you know, when they can . And they’re able, but you know, Clare Valley is always going to be a Riesling region. They’re not trying to reinvent the wheel, but they are looking at what they can do to make more modern styles of wine with the varieties and the places and the climate that they have.

SV

Yeah, I mean, in terms of Australian wine climates, you know, as you were speaking, Tasmania came to mind too, as like another cool climate region. Obviously, it’s very near Antarctica. Are the styles.. I mean, I know the varieties and everything is different. Are the styles or types of Clare Valley wines comparable? Is there any sort of comparison between those and Tasmanian wines, or is there another region in Australia that you could compare Clare Valley style to?

CP

Oh, that’s an interesting question. I would say not so much Tasmania. Tasmania is super cool, so they are really focused on sparkling and a little bit of Riesling, a little bit of cool climate, Shiraz, but really sparkling in Pinot Noir, and some great Chardonnay too, so they’re kind of their own beast down there. What they’re doing, I would say, to compare maybe someplace like Heath Kit, one of the regions, so in Victoria, which is where Melbourne is. So really, just like if you keep driving east from Adelaide, you’ll get to Melbourne eventually, you’ll drive through McLaren Vale, you’ll get into all these like Fleurieu Peninsula, into Kunawara, which is very well known for its Cabs, so then, and then eventually, if you keep driving, you’ll get to Melbourne, and so then in Victoria has all these small regions with pretty, it’s pretty like quality over quantity there, so it’s small producers making pretty high-end wines, and that really, most of Victoria is what I would consider a cool climate. Some have cooler climates than others. They change, but maybe comparing Clare, I would say someplace like Heathkit. No, actually, even Heath Kit is bigger style, maybe like Beechworth, which is up in the mountains, up in the Southern Alps, right toward north, north of Melbourne, basically, where you still get this power, and you can, you can make quite concentrated wines, like they, they have that Aussie sunshine there, they have no problem with ripeness. Most of Australia has no problem with ripening their grapes, unlike, you know, places like New York, for example, where they, they do have vintages where it’s really tough, they’re tough to ripen grapes there, they’ve got, you know, ease, abundance of sunshine almost everywhere in Oz, so you get that concentration and you get that real core of fruit, but still, because of the elevation, those nighttime, daytime temperatures that really retains that acidity and keeps that freshness, so I think that that interesting kind of juxtaposition between power and freshness is what I see coming from Clare and coming from Beechworth, maybe, but in a different, a different way. They’re still their own things, and that’s why I love really geeking out on Oz, because no two regions are really the same. There’s a really big diversity across Australian wine, and that’s why you know Australia surprised me when I first moved there, and maybe it did for you too. Aussies, like, for all their like beach bum, laid-back culture, are very serious about wine, and the wine knowledge, the bar is high there, like, they, there are a lot of people very serious about it, and they know their wines, and they know other wines outside their country too, but they really, they really know wine there in a way that I sometimes, yeah, I miss, I miss not living there anymore.

SV

Yeah, I mean, I can’t say my first time around living there I was 20, turning 21 and my preference was whatever was cheap, so that meant a lot of box wine, but –

CP

They did invent the bag-in-box.

SV

I was very familiar with it during my time there, but I was, I will say, surprised when I was going back and visiting my friends and friends of friends who, you know, I hung out with 20 years ago, and one of them is very into wine, apparently needs a bigger wine cellar. He has wine stashed under his bed in his closet, much to his wife’s chagrin. But he was sharing, he was the one that first told me about Wendouree and was sharing a bunch of wines with me, and winery tips. I was very impressed by how the adults who I was hanging out with, how educated they were on Australian wine in general.

CP

Yeah, and you know, they have a big country to explore, just, but they also, yeah, they also have a pretty good knowledge of, like, French wines, not so much American wines, and it’s like vice versa, like we don’t have great knowledge as Americans, we don’t drink a lot of Aussie wines, they don’t get a ton of American wines over there, so you know it does work both ways. Yeah, right now the dollar is not in their favor either. 

SV

So, I want to kind of circle back to Wendouree and its place in the international market. I know you can’t get it here. Is it a wine that you think would do well in the States, or do you think it’s like a part of its popularity is just a product of like Australian pride? Where do you think it sits internationally?

CP

I think if it was here and Americans knew the story and the romanticism and the scarcity that it probably would do great, because Americans love, you know, love something they can’t get a hold of. Why I wanted it so badly. Yeah, right. As soon as they know they can’t have it, like I want it. So I think that’s a very American mentality that I’m sure would do it, would do very well here for that reason, but I, you know, I don’t think it’s ever going to be here. They have no reason to export it. The worrying thing, I think, for a lot of us is there doesn’t seem to be any legacy plan in place with Wendouree, and Lita and Tony are both in their 70s, and you know, I don’t think, as far as I know, I could be wrong. Maybe somebody will call in and prove me wrong, but as far as I know, there’s not anybody to take over. It really is a.. it’s. it’s Tony and Lita who are Wendorie, so that is slightly sad to me. We just had a similar thing happen with a winemaker in England, Peter Hall, who was just this enigmatic, incredible English winemaker, who you know everyone was like, we had, he has no legacy, and he recently passed away, but that actually has a happy ending, in that now it turns out one of.. I actually don’t know all the details, but it sounds like a few of his kids are stepping up to keep his wineries called Breaky Bottom are stepping up to keep that going, and I think there’s been a big sigh of relief with, you know, in the wine industry for going, “Oh, good, because we can’t imagine a world without Breaky Bottom in it.” I think similarly the the Aussies can’t imagine a world without Wendouree in it, so it would be very sad to see it go. It’s such a magical place, but I think for what it represents in Australian wine and in the wine world as a whole, we’ll treasure it for as long as we, as long as we have it.

SV

Amazing. Well, thank you so much, Christina, for filling us in on all things Australian wine, Clare Valley, Wendouree. As of right now, it sounds like the best way to taste it is to just go to the Clare Valley, super easy.

CP

Ask nicely, butter up to people. I mean, you can call and get on their mailing list, and maybe in a couple of decades you might, might get a bottle or two, have it shipped to an Australian friend, or you can go to the local pub hotel, you know, as they call it,

SV

And try a bottle there. Otherwise, I think you’re probably out of luck,

CP

But there are so many other fantastic Aussie wines. Please drink Aussie wine, explore it. There is a whole world of wine waiting for you to enjoy, so lots of delicious stuff out there from land down under. 

SV

Americans and Australians may share a lot of cultural similarities, but the wine crossover hasn’t yet reached critical mass. Have you had a chance to experience any of the country’s boutique wines or higher-end bottle lengths? If so, we’d love to know what you thought. You can email us your comments and questions at podcasts at Wine enthusias.com and feel free to check out Pickard’s expansive buying guide to some of her favorite Australian wines that can be found in the US, and feel free to check out Pickard’s expansive buying guide to some of her favorite Australian wines that be found in the US on our website. Also, remember you can subscribe to this podcast on Apple, Spotify, and anywhere else you listen to your favorite shows. Can also go to Wineenthusiast.com/podcast for more episodes and transcripts. I’m Sara Ventiera. Thanks for listening. Thanks.

title: 8 Wineries Around the World That Are Basically Sculpture Museums
summary:
Pause to swirl your glass while admiring the striking sculptures at these wineries.

The post 8 Wineries Around the World That Are Basically Sculpture Museums appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
It’s not uncommon for people who love wine to also love art.  So, it’s only natural that wineries around the world are serving fresh-air wine and sculpture pairings, with on-site sculpture gardens, outdoor galleries, and artful walking trails unfurling through the vines. For the visitor, these sip, stroll, and sculpture experiences become multi-sensory explorations of the interplay between land and art and wine, celebrating the creativity of both the winemaker and the artist. 

Meander through these eight wineries near and far that showcase both wine and sculpture.

Pt. Leo Estate

Merricks, Victoria, Australia

The owners of Mornington Peninsula’s Pt. Leo Estate share their personal collection of large-scale sculptures in an ever-evolving, 16-acre Sculpture Park, which welcomes approximately 200,000 visitors each year. The outdoor gallery boasts a stellar group of 70-plus permanent, large-scale works by Australian and international artists including Yayoi Kusama, Henry Moore, Reko Rennie, Ugo Rondinone, Jaume Plensa, Barry Flanagan, Inge King, Antony Gormley, and KAWS. While enjoying sweeping Southern Ocean vistas, guests can try a glass of Pt. Leo Estate’s Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, or Pinot Gris before joining a guided tour of the sculptures.

Château de Vullierens

Vullierens, Switzerland

Château de Vullierens has belonged to the same family for over 700 years, and these days it’s also a certified biodynamic vineyard featuring an open-air sculpture park with over 120 works of contemporary art woven into the estate’s 74 acres of gardens, forests, and vineyards. Bonus: The whole thing overlooks Lake Geneva and the Alps. There’s even an interactive sculpture trail designed just for kids. “Both wine and sculpture invite discovery, curiosity, and exchange,” says Julie Haering, project manager at Château de Vullierens. “Just as each wine expresses a terroir, each artwork interacts with the landscape, the light, the seasons, and the visitor’s personal interpretation.”

The Donum Estate

Sonoma, USA

Wine, sustainable agriculture, and art unite within the cohesive vision of The Donum Estate, where the world-class Donum Collection includes over 60 monumental works displayed over 200 acres of land. Visitors are encouraged to engage with nature (and sip Donum Chardonnay and Pinot Noir) while they peruse sculptures by renowned artists including Keith Haring, Yayoi Kusama, Ai Weiwei, Robert Indiana, Anselm Kiefer, Fernando Botero, and Louise Bourgeois. A range of immersive tour experiences, from guided walking to all-terrain vehicles, are the perfect way to see Donum’s culinary gardens, working farm, and art collection.

Brick Bay

Matakana, New Zealand

Up for a walk? In the Matakana wine region of New Zealand, Brick Bay offers a two-kilometer Sculpture Trail. It’s an open-air exhibition of contemporary New Zealand art, with an ever-changing collection of over 60 large-scale sculptures scattered across a natural landscape with vineyards, lakes, open pastures, a kauri forest, and native bush. While tasting Brick Bay’s Pinot Gris, Chardonnay, rosé, or Bordeaux blends, guests might hear the birdsong of a kākā or tūī as they roam among sculptures that move, float, or interact with the sun and wind.

Exclusive Access to
Wine Country,
Led by Wine Enthusiast

Artelium Wine Estate

East Sussex, England

Art is in the DNA of Artelium Wine Estate, where visitors encounter a growing collection of contemporary sculpture displayed within 45 verdant acres on the edge of the South Downs. “We wanted to create a place where wine, art, and nature could exist together, so the sculpture program evolved very naturally from that vision,” says Julie Bretland, Artelium co-founder. “Wine is such a cultural product—it’s shaped by creativity, craftsmanship, patience, and place—and sculpture offers another way for people to connect with that.” Along with permanent sculpture installations, the winery hosts temporary exhibitions and supports artist collaborations, an artist-in-residence program, and even stone carving workshops.

Montalto

Red Hill South, Victoria, Australia

Visitors to Montalto’s 1.2-kilometer Sculpture Trail are invited to grab a glass of wine and take off through the vines, into the woods, and along wetland boardwalks to discover a rotating collection of over 40 contemporary works of sculpture. “There’s a beautiful resonance between sculpture and the work of the vineyard,” says Simon Black, Montalto’s chief winemaker. “You’re tasting the organic creativity of the winemaker and encountering the organic creativity of the sculptors at the same time, and every sense is engaged: the sight of the landscape, the scent of the gardens and the vines, the taste of the wine.”

Paradise Ridge Winery

Sonoma, USA

At Paradise Ridge, guests can savor award-winning wines and Russian River Valley vistas before taking a walk through Marijke’s Grove, a four-acre sculpture garden launched in 1996 that features both a permanent collection and an ongoing schedule of rotating shows. Large-scale pieces include David Best’s 25-ton “Temple of Remembrance” and Laura Kimpton’s 12-foot-tall “LOVE” sculpture. Fun fact: The winery also gives a second life to artworks that previously debuted at Burning Man.

“Wine is one way that people connect with the land, and sculpture is another,” says Paradise Ridge’s Maria Juliana Byck. “At its heart, the sculpture program is about creating a place where people can enjoy nature, art, and wine together in a way that feels contemplative, inspiring, and rejuvenating.”

Château La Coste

Provence, France

With a winery and welcome building designed by Jean Nouvel and Tadao Ando, respectively, Château La Coste is a treasure trove of artistic brilliance. Along with olive groves, a forest, vegetable garden, and vineyard, the 494-acre property houses 45 sculpture installations, including both temporary and permanent exhibitions. On a guided two-hour Art & Architecture Walk, visitors meander over hills, through the woods, and into the vineyards while admiring four kilometers of creative expression, including works by Louise Bourgeois, Frank Gehry, Yoko Ono, Tracey Emin, Ai Weiwei, Richard Serra, Sophie Calle, Kengo Kuma, and even an ironwork freight car by Bob Dylan.

  • These U.K. pubs double as a cozy place to rest your head.

  • You should take the kids on your next wine trip. Seriously.
  • Prince Edward County is the wine destination you didn’t know you needed.

title: How to Stand Out in a Bouquet of Rosés
summary:
Winemakers are using playful and inventive bottle shapes to distinguish themselves on a shelf full of pink.

The post How to Stand Out in a Bouquet of Rosés appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Other than its namesake blush coloring, the limits on what constitutes rosé are few: neither specific regions nor particular grapes necessarily define the category, and myriad winemaking techniques can result in pink juice. Certain regions are, of course, well-known for rosé, including Provence and France’s rosé-only Tavel appellation, and many winemakers are indeed producing varietally-designated rosés. For a category that has been gaining rapid traction since #RoséAllDay was born over a decade ago, however, it seems most consumers pay little attention to what rosé actually is—other than refreshing and pink.

“When I speak to consumers, nobody knows [how it’s made], and it’s not very important for them, I think,” says Olivier Souvelain, CEO of Maison Gassier in Provence. “Everyone is surprised that it’s not simply a blend between red and white.” 

“I’m not sure most consumers fully understand how rosé is made,” concurs Emma Bertrand, creative director of Gerard Bertrand’s Cote des Roses. “But is that really necessary to enjoy it? Today, rosé is less about technical knowledge and more about what it represents: a lifestyle, a mood, a destination,” she says. “I think people don’t necessarily choose a rosé for its grape variety, but for how it makes them feel.” 

This thinking is consistent with evidence presented during a recent Wine Paris session entitled “How Consumers Choose a Bottle of Wine: What Neuroscience Reveals About Packaging.” Isabelle Gruard, of packaging leader Crealis, and neuroscientist Andrea Ciceri, of data analytics resource Sensecatch, shared statistics that speak to this very phenomenon: research shows 95% of daily decisions are driven by the emotional system of the brain, while only 5% are based on rational thinking. Consumers spend all of about 15 seconds in front of a shelf making a decision, with any given product only getting a few fleeting seconds to capture attention.

Grabbing Attention with Rosé-Colored Glasses

Given that rosé is such a lifestyle-driven category, this feelings-driven decision-making is perhaps more relevant than with any other wine style. “Rosé is inherently visual,” says Max Rohn, CEO of Wölffer Estate Vineyard. “It’s about color and emotion, so the bottle becomes an extension of that experience.”

“In more traditional wine segments, consumers often navigate by appellation, producer reputation, or vintage,” Riccardo Pasqua, CEO of Pasqua Wines, points out. “In rosé, those markers carry less weight for most buyers. What drives the decision is far more instinctive—a shape that catches the eye, a label that provokes curiosity, or a name that sticks.”

On that score, savvy rosé producers are using enhanced visual or mnemonic cues beyond color to stand out in an increasingly crowded field. “Rosé is one of the most competitive and visually-driven categories in wine,” says Rohn. “On a shelf full of pale pink, differentiation becomes critical.”

Producers use this notion to their advantage. Wölffer Estate’s flower-adorned rosé, rebranded to Summer in a Bottle, has grown from just over 1500 cases in 2014 to tens of thousands of cases annually. Pasqua’s shapely and playfully-named 11 Minutes represents 10% of the Veneto-based brand’s total profile, with its strongest sales among on-trade, lifestyle-driven settings like hotel bars and beach clubs. “[These are settings] where the bottle has a life beyond the shelf,” says Pasqua. “It sits on a table and other guests see it; it travels around a room.”

Distinctive in both bottle design and name, Bertrand’s Cote des Roses “has become one of the most recognizable French rosé brands internationally, particularly in the U.S.,” says Bertrand, with sales figures that approach the perennially popular Château d’Esclans Whispering Angel. Finally, Maison Gassier’s sales of its estate rosé grew by over 40% in 2025, with all other factors being equal year-over-year, simply by rolling out a new hexagonal bottle shape.

Silhouettes in Rosé

Bottle shape has always played a significant part in the rosé category, especially in Provence, with the introduction of various come-hither silhouettes. Most notably, the consortium Syndicat de Défense des Côtes de Provence (now known as AOC Côtes de Provence) introduced a signature bottle for the region in the 1960s: the voluptuous “flûte à corset Provençal.”

Such bottles acted not only as markers of differentiation, but also as signifiers of quality. “Provençal roses are some of the most expensive all over the world,” acknowledges Souvelain. “It’s like perfume. When you sell something expensive, you try to prove [visually] that what’s inside is of a different quality than what others are offering.”

At Wine Paris, the Provençal rosé section alone revealed dozens of bottle shapes, even among wines with a generally similar makeup. It’s no wonder that even legacy producers such as 50-year-old Gassier would want to explore a new look. Its new hexagonal bottle is about more than just style, though—it tells a story, with six sides meant to reflect the brand’s six generations of winemakers. The bottle also shoulders the burden of price justification with the new design’s implied added value. “Before we launched the new bottle we did a study with 2,000 consumers between France and the U.S.,” says Souvelain. “In both markets, nearly everyone surveyed said that they would agree to pay even just one euro or one dollar more per bottle for the new shape.”

Meanwhile, Pasqua’s bottle features broad shoulders compared to most in the rosé category, but without excessive flourish. “We wanted a silhouette that felt distinctive and premium without being decorative for its own sake,” says Pasqua. And Cote des Roses’ bottle, distinctive in its heavy-bottomed, rose-embossed punt and glass cork, “creates a very strong in-store interaction,” says Bertrand. “People notice it, pick it up, and when they enjoy the wine, they easily recognize it the next time. That physical connection naturally drives repeat purchases. It’s a wine that people don’t just drink, they share it, photograph it, and gift it.”

title: Go Ahead—Judge Wine by Its Label
summary:
Wine labels are becoming more Instagrammable, expressive, and fun as producers catch up to what young drinkers want.

The post Go Ahead—Judge Wine by Its Label appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
After a string of TikToks touted La Vieille Ferme Rosé in 2024, Famille Perrin, owners of Château de Beaucastel, were faced with a conundrum. Consumers—particularly newer, younger ones—didn’t know how to pronounce the name of their wine. Instead, internet minions were calling the decades-old brand—which has earned widespread recognition for being an approachable, inexpensive and consistently well-made rosé—“chicken wine,” so-named because of the chicken illustration on the label.

But rather than letting it bristle their feathers, the makers leaned in. A few months later, they launched a new limited-edition label. The words “La Vieille Ferme Rosé” are nowhere to be found; in their place, “The Chicken Wine” is prominently emblazoned. The rebrand has so far delighted TikTok users across the U.S. and U.K., who have embraced the new label.

“Wine can still be such a scary, antiquated ‘white tablecloth’ category for so many people,” says Sande Friedman, a wine writer based in Philadelphia. “Younger consumers are far more vocal about that than previous generations … and admit openly that we shop with our eyes and are more open to change than previous generations may have been. A generation of people shopping for ‘the chicken wine’ really showed that power.”

The Power of Visuals

Indeed, the Chicken Wine phenomenon proves, once again, that visuals matter, whether we want to admit it or not. In turn, wine manufacturers are placing new weight on what goes on a label, from the illustrations and typeface to wine descriptors and buzzwords.

“Instructional buzzwords can actually help inform customers of how the winemaker would enjoy this product themselves,” says Friedman. “‘Chillable’ is a word you see a lot … that helps inform people that red wine can be chilled.”

And colorful labels with playful fonts aren’t just frivolous details. Much like a shop’s window dressing can lure in foot traffic, these eye-catching labels can be the difference between making a sale and, well, not. Consumer research backs this up, suggesting that both colors and fonts play significant roles in reeling in new customers. “There’s been an undeniable shift in the look of wine labels in recent years,” Friedman says. “It’s in tandem with what you see happening in soda, snacks, all the ‘fun’ food categories. [There are] bright colors, buzzwords, flash and pomp.”

A Generational Shift

The shift in buying habits is notedly generational. For many millennial and Gen Z consumers, demand for wine has shifted, softened, and evolved in a post-pandemic timeline, says Kathryn Klos, a North Carolina-based sommelier turned travel advisor specializing in wine tourism. 

“For younger generations, who are often less wealthy, experienced and perhaps less ‘serious’ about wine, a playful or artful label signals approachability,” she says. “They know their friends or followers might not know the difference between Montepulciano and Brunello di Montalcino, but they sure as hell know a cute label.”

Klos says this isn’t to minimize the wine knowledge of younger generations. “But the truth is, fewer of us can afford to drink wine as a serious hobby the way older generations could,” she stresses. From a stocking perspective, listening to this customer feedback is critical for winemakers looking to stay relevant.

“It’s a very important driver in the current market,” says Friedman, explaining that some importers are supporting the design of tiny producers’ fun labels to help little cuvées from native grapes stand out.

It’s also convincing more makers to incorporate UPC barcodes into their labels, which can help streamline inventory management and more accurately track products throughout the supply chain. “Big, huge distributors are noticing and copying, which is why you see this everywhere now,” she says.

New Styles, New Label Best Practices

Natural wines—a broad category without a firm definition—are leading the modern label charge. One possible explanation: Many producers, especially those in Europe, are legally prohibited from naming the wine’s regional designation or the specific vineyard in which it was made. (These wines are often deemed “atypical” for their region and are excluded from these designations.) 

Without being able to capitalize on such selling points, many winemakers instead lean on untraditional, eye-catching branding. Case in point, the delicate, floral- inspired labels from Jurtschitsch, a natural wine producer in Austria’s Kamptal region, and the whimsical, cartoonish labels from Domaine de l’Octavin, a Demeter biodynamic-certified outfit in the Arbois region of the Jura.

There’s plenty of great natural wine out there, made by vintners with high-quality standards. But as hype for natural wine continues to trend, shelves are becoming saturated with natural wines of varying quality, many of them bearing flashy labels slapped with phrases like “low sulfur,” “naked,” and “sugar-free.” 

Critics say these health-adjacent buzzwords are unhelpful at best and misleading at worst. There are scant legal regulations around their use, which, coupled with sexy label art, can confuse consumers, particularly those unfamiliar with classic winemaking. As a result, more faulty wines with off-putting flavors or aromas are entering the marketplace.

“There was a moment in natural wine when labels alone could get you on a shelf and sell bottles,” says Klos. “And unfortunately, the wine inside was bad more often than it should have been.”

The Good vs. Bad of New Labels

So, does the good outweigh the bad in the great label debate? Friedman says cool branding can help mask cruddy wine, but it can also push boundaries in an industry that’s often stuck in its ways.

“Anything that makes people more comfortable shopping is a win for an industry that’s been in a downward trend in recent years,” she says. “It also actually helps people remember wines they’ve enjoyed better, because there’s an easy visual instead of trying to remember a grape or place.”

Plus, Klos says that a fun label means a consumer is more likely to deem a bottle “gram worthy” and perhaps give it some free marketing online. “More often than not, a nice bottle is a splurge—and one that deserves to be documented on social media,” she says. “The game has changed for the next generation of wine drinkers, and art has a lot of power in that game.”

What Makes a Good Label?

Wine consultant Kathryn Klos shares what makes for an effective label these days.

Buzzwords Beyond Buzzwords’ Sake

“When there’s full transparency from a winemaker or brand about farming practices, regenerative choices and winemaking intention, that’s a beautiful thing. But when big brands use unregulated phrases like ‘no chemicals added’ without doing anything different just to make more money, we run into problems.”

Aesthetically Pleasing, but Authentic

“Yes, the label should be art-forward, but art without purpose feels empty and people can sniff that out. There’s nothing wrong with beauty, but when art becomes detached from intention, it risks losing its soul. A wine label can be wild and whimsical, but if it doesn’t echo the spirit of what’s inside, it becomes costume instead of character.”

Storytelling with Honesty

“Just like a great wine reflects its maker and its place, good label copy is an opportunity to extend the story and make the experience more memorable. Ultimately, the intended audience and their values should dictate what that means.”

This article originally appeared in the June/July 2025 California issue of Wine Enthusiast magazine. Click here to subscribe today!

title: These 15 Frozen Cocktails Prove That Slushies Aren’t Just for Kids
summary:
A piña colada by the pool? A sophisticated slushie after dinner? Or a frozen espresso martini to get the night started? Take your pick with our favorite frozen cocktail recipes.

The post These 15 Frozen Cocktails Prove That Slushies Aren’t Just for Kids appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Frozen drinks can be polarizing. To a certain extent, the haters have a point. There are many ill-fated iterations of slushies that range from boozy and headache-inducing to icy and cloyingly sweet. But we’re here to tell you it doesn’t have to be this way. 

When done right, frozen drinks are the quintessential grown-up slushie. The best are supremely refreshing while packing a punch. Plus, they can easily be tweaked to personal preferences for strength, sweetness, consistency, and acidity. In the summertime, they are an obvious choice for all poolside, seaside, or rooftop occasions and beyond. 

But even in the depths of winter, these cool cocktails can transport us to fantasies of warm weather and sunshine. Contrary to popular belief, frozen cocktails don’t have to be tropical and fruit-forward. There are plenty of recipes that lean into the milkshake vibes, and they are satisfyingly decadent in any season. 

We’ve rounded up our favorite frozen drink recipes for when you need a little something special to sip on. Yes, they’re a little indulgent—but all the best things in life are.

The Classics

Piña Colada

The piña colada is a classic for a reason. White, dark, and coconut rums meet pineapple juice and coconut cream for an easy-drinking slush that’ll make you feel like you’re on vacation no matter the time or place.

Frozen Painkiller

The Painkiller adds a splash of orange juice to the classic piña colada flavor profile for a citrusy hit, and this frozen variation is as tropical and dreamy as can be.

Frozen Hemingway Daiquiri

The frozen Hemingway Daiquiri just might be as tiki as it gets. With two types of rum and a smidge of Maraschino liqueur, it’s lightly sweet and perfectly boozy. Plus—the addition of grapefruit juice means it’s practically made for breakfast or brunch.

Frozen Gin & Tonics

This recipe for frozen G&Ts uses tonic syrup to replicate the classic tonic water flavors. It’s bitter, lightly sweet, and ultra refreshing with a touch of citrus.

Brandy Slush

You don’t need to be in Wisconsin to enjoy the classic brandy slush with this no-frills, no-blender recipe. With a little planning ahead and a combination of brandy, fruit juices, and black tea, this midwest aperitivo is served.

The Twists

Baller Margarita

This frozen margarita variation also nods to the Sgroppino with the addition of lime sherbet and Champagne (although any sparkling wine will do). A splash of Licor 43 adds sweetness and spice.

San Jose Frosé

The San Jose frosé blitzes up rosé with frozen lemonade cubes and lemon juice for a tart twist on the classic frozen rosé. 

Whiskey Sour Slush

Love whiskey sours but don’t love using raw egg whites at home? Want something a bit better suited for the sweltering summer? This whiskey sour slush is the answer. Plus—no blender required!

Frozen Strawberry Riesling Cocktail

Listen, if you don’t like sweet drinks, you might want to skip this strawberry and riesling slush, as it definitely leans into the fruitier side of things. Still, the strawberry and lime paired with the delicate Riesling is almost irresistible.

Sophisticated Slushie

This sophisticated slushie blends the tequila and grapefruit flavors of a Paloma with the texture and sparkling wine technique of an Italian Sgroppino. It’s bright and refreshing—the perfect sipper all summer long.

Ube Colada

Kara Newman describes this Ube Colada as “a piña colada with a Filipino accent.” The purple yam’s vanilla flavors meld harmoniously with the piña colada’s pineapple-coconut profile. 

Jack Frost Cocktail

Sometimes, looks can be deceiving, as is the case with this tropical and fruity Jack Frost cocktail. It may look festive and creamy due to the blue Curaçao and snow-like coconut rim, but, with rum, pineapple juice, and coconut water, it’s actually more like a piña colada in holiday mode. 

The Boozy Milkshakes

Grand Cayman Mudslide

The Mudslide just might be the most essential, creamy frozen drink. Equal parts of vodka (vanilla-flavored, if you’re feeling fancy), Kahlua, and Baileys are blitzed together to a sweet and luscious treat. 

The Bushwacker

The Bushwacker is a delicious marriage between a White Russian, piña colada, and mudslide. It might sound like a lot of flavors at once, but the creamy coconut and coffee liqueur balance each other well. Try out vodka in place of the rum or a splash of orange liqueur for a bright twist.

Frozen Espresso Martini

The simple blend of vodka, coffee, and simple syrup keeps this frozen espresso cocktail refreshing and straightforward. To make it even more decadent, add a bit of Baileys.

title: Will New York City’s New Anti-Alcohol Campaign Actually Empty Glasses?
summary:
The controversial campaign to educate New Yorkers about the negative health risks of wine, beer, and cocktails is alarming hospitality pros.

The post Will New York City’s New Anti-Alcohol Campaign Actually Empty Glasses? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Early in May, a new type of beer poster was wheatpasted on New York City’s bus stops, subway platforms, bar walls, and bathrooms. They even popped up on Instagram feeds. But these ads weren’t for Heineken or Brooklyn Brewery. Instead, the featured beer labels read, “Cancer Risk”.

Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s new campaign, “Buzzkill,” is aimed at educating New Yorkers about the risks associated with alcohol consumption—some studies have found there is strong evidence that correlates several forms of cancer and heavy alcohol consumption.

“New Yorkers deserve to know the truth about the risk of cancer when drinking alcohol,” says Dr. Alister Martin, New York City’s newly-appointed Health Commissioner. 

But industry members find the campaign alarmist and, frankly, scary as hell—specifically, using the c-word to get New Yorkers on the wagon. Moreso, critics argue the ads favor fear-mongering reports, and forgo the scientific nuance of alcohol-and-health arguments. 

Are scare tactics the right approach for getting New Yorkers to engage with alcohol more responsibly? And will these posters impact the already-slim margins of the city’s struggling hospitality industry? 

But the biggest question is, is this a sign of similar policies to come?

Unpacking the Policies

The campaign’s primary message is: alcohol is a known carcinogen, and drinking less will lower your risk of cancer. 

“To some our data may be a buzzkill, but it’s actually their buzz that can kill,” says Dr. Martin’s statement. “The good news is that drinking less lowers the risk of cancer, and knowing the facts means you’re in control.”

It’s the first time any major American city has used cancer—not drinking and driving, underage drinking, or general debauchery—as their temperance campaign’s central focus.

Other international cities and countries have pursued similar efforts.

Ireland has warnings on cans and bottles connecting alcohol and fatal cancers. South Australia plastered subway cars with glasses of red wine, with the accompanying text “alcohol causes cancer in seven sites of the body.”

Mamdani’s campaign announcement went even further, and acted as a bouncing-off point for State Senator Nathalia Fernandez to advocate for a tax on alcohol, which will fund prevention, education, and rehabilitation efforts. 

When you compare Mayor Mamdani’s campaign with the current Trump administration’s guidance, things get complicated. 

The 2025 to 2030 U.S. Dietary Guidelines, released earlier this year by the Trump administration, scrapped the recommendations that women and men should have no more than one, and two, drinks per day, respectively.

Moreover, recent research from the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine (NASEM), which was used to inform the Dietary Guidelines, found that “moderate alcohol consumption” can lower risks of cardiovascular disease and all-cause mortality. However, the report did connect moderate alcohol consumption with a higher risk of breast cancer. 

Meanwhile, the World Health Organization (WHO) takes an opposite stance, stating that no amount of alcohol is safe for human consumption. Conflicting and confusing? Yes, but don’t worry: We’ve catalogued the full breakdown of health and alcohol’s ongoing facts and theories.

What Experts Think

Of course, industry experts agree that it is important consumers are aware of potential health risks. 

Excessive drinking is highly dangerous, hard stop. The New York State Department of Health estimates over 8,050 New Yorkers die each year due to excessive alcohol use, and one in six adults across the state participate in binge or heavy drinking. 

Alcohol consumption can nominally raise your risk for several different types of cancer by 1 to 2%, as do genetics, diet, smoking, and other environmental factors.

But, critics take issue with how the campaign stuck the landing. The posters flag that alcohol—all alcohol—has cancer risks. It’s a binary argument with a dark insinuation: Your evening plans, whether that’s 20 beers with the boys or a glass of wine with dinner, could have terminal outcomes.

“There’s a wide gap between encouraging mindful consumption and a campaign like this built on loosely-interpreted facts to create fear,” says David Skurnik, president of Skurnik Wines & Spirits.

Colin Spoelman, founder and distiller of Kings County Distillery, agrees, saying, “There’s no sense of the relative harm of alcohol in these posters. Is it bad for you like smoking? Like hot dogs? Like sugary drinks?”

Spoelman doesn’t understand why alcohol is the local villain here, while the aforementioned vices are all equally antagonized by the World Health Organization. Why is New York putting drinks in its crosshairs?

“If the mayor won’t lay out the harms of hot dogs for New York City residents, then he is playing favorites,” says Spoelman.

Torrey Grant, wine director at Leonetta and The Derby Club, seconds this, and points out that the increased relative risk of colorectal cancer from two glasses of wine per day is 7%. 50 grams of processed meat—about the size of a standard hot dog—will increase relative risk by 18%.

“Can you imagine going after the BEC or a pastrami sandwich at Katz Deli as a carcinogen!?” says Grant. “The BEC is fuel for the working New Yorker. The Katz Pastrami is a foundational food for this city. But it doesn’t absolve them of being carcinogenic, does it?“

It’s not that these industry pros are against health warnings, per se—they’re for fairness. “I would favor updating federal health warning statements to be more specific,” says Spoelman. “But at a city level, it seems misplaced.”

Given the backlash, it begs the question: Is this really the right public health battle for the mayor’s office to tackle?

“I believe that NY’s new ‘anti-buzz’ campaign is a misdirected effort to improve the health of NY’ers—which, in itself, is a valid goal,” says Wendy Weinstein of The Newport Restaurant Group. “There are greater threats to everyday health and risk of long term disease for New Yorkers: omnipresent bad food options, food scarcity, nutritional education, and food deserts.”

Rather than “the pinpointing of micro factors,” she believes, “a comprehensive overhaul of the foodways of many NY’ers is needed to combat cancer.”

Glasses Half Empty

Both the restaurant and alcohol industries are suffering through myriad challenges, like ever-slim margins, supply chain issues, and changing consumer preferences, and it’s natural to think that this campaign might make matters worse.

Gillian Garrett is an alcohol regulatory attorney. She agrees the campaigns are alarmist, and she expects them to lead to a decrease in alcohol consumption. 

“When people are told a product causes cancer, they use less of it,” says Garrett. 

Elia Chuaqui, a sommelier at Hudson Local, expects to see that reflected in sales, especially if Senator Fernandez’ tax is to move forward. To Chuaqui, affordability will worsen, resulting in selling fewer glasses per visit, while having to focus on more value-driven selections and a wider array of by-the-glass options.

“In the short term, we would expect some softening in demand,” says Chuaqui. “Long term, the impact is harder to predict.” 

Wine consumption is deeply social, especially in New York. People come to New York for the pedigree of bars, restaurants, and culture. 

“Dining, hospitality, tourism, and small businesses are such an important part of New York’s community fabric,” says Skurnik. “Responsible wine consumption as a shared experience is a big part of what makes the city special and vibrant.” 

Here is where the problem lies: “Reducing wine to a public health warning ignores its enormous contribution to our cultural values,” he adds.
